- Checksum validation caught quality issues before production in each region
- Absorbed compliance-driven delays in Asia and Switzerland without derailing overall timeline
- Sales teams gained clean unified data on single platform, replacing limited proprietary system
Process: - Led data cleansing of proprietary legacy system across regions
- Designed tranche-based migration starting US-first then rolling outward
- Built repeatable scripted migration with checksum validation at each stage
- Navigated MAS, FSA, FSC/FSS, FINMA compliance requirements that delayed Asia and Switzerland rollouts
- Re-sequenced go-lives around compliance blockers while keeping other regions moving
5PSummary: JP Morgan's Asset Management sales organization needed to migrate from a limited proprietary legacy system to Salesforce across 12 countries — with messy data and multi-jurisdictional compliance requirements in Asia and Switzerland painful enough to delay rollouts. I designed a tranche-based migration starting US-first, built checksum validation to catch issues before production, and re-sequenced around compliance blockers. Delivered successful migration across all 12 countries, giving sales teams clean unified data on a single platform for the first time.

Title: Discovery & Framing - De-Risking Product Development at Fortune 500 Scale
Project: Cloud Innovation Center - Product Development Practice
Use Case(s): De-risk product investment where ambitious ideas lack clarity on user needs, technical feasibility, and business value. Apply structured Discovery & Framing methodology to validate assumptions before committing to full-scale development.
Interview Questions: Tell me about formalizing Discovery & Framing as a core methodology across Fortune 500 engagements — what problem were you solving and how did you operationalize it?, Walk me through the JP Morgan legal engagement — how did Discovery & Framing redirect an engagement where the client was confident they already knew the solution?, How did you adapt Discovery & Framing across different contexts like Kaiser condition management and American Express digital experiences?, Describe how you trained 150+ practitioners to apply Discovery & Framing consistently — what did that training look like in practice?, How did Discovery & Framing change the way clients approached product development after engagements ended?
Situation: Clients consistently pushed back on structured discovery — "we know what we want, just start building." Traditional Accenture delivery teams reinforced this by defaulting to requirements gathering and jumping to development. The result was predictable: misaligned products, late-stage rework, and scope disputes that eroded client trust. Discovery & Framing had to earn its place by proving that 4-8 weeks of upfront alignment saved months of wrong-direction development.

JP Morgan's legal team had spent two years trying to solve a case management problem. When they engaged the CIC, leadership was confident in the solution direction and wanted to move straight to design and build. Once our team conducted user research with the actual attorneys, we discovered the underlying problem was different from what had been assumed — and redirected the engagement.
Task: Establish Discovery & Framing as a core Cloud Innovation Center practice that validated user needs, technical feasibility, and business value before committing to build — enabling clients to invest confidently in the right solutions.
Action: - current state review, stakeholder and user interviews, technology deep dives, user journey mapping, persona development, and POC testing. Discovery culminated in scenario stories representing the emotional and JTBD ("jobs to be done") context of how users actually interact with the product — not what leadership assumed they needed

- user journey mapping tied to scenario stories, feature shaping and prioritization, success metrics (customer, business, performance), rapid prototyping with lo-fi interactive mockups, tight test-and-iterate loops, and value vs. implementation matrix. Framing culminated in a prioritized backlog backed by interactive prototypes and user stories — aligned to a unified vision delivering day-one value

- Paired 1:1 with client across disciplines to teach lean development methods — Extreme Programming, user-centered design, and lean product management — so the client could sustain the practices after the engagement

- Kaiser condition management (clinician interviews, care plan mapping starting with diabetes, rolling D&F for additional conditions), American Express (surfaced competing stakeholder priorities, aligned through OKRs), pharma (validated hypotheses with medical professionals and donors)

- Documented the methodology into reusable templates, facilitation guides, and training materials. Trained 150+ CIC practitioners. Approach became Accenture standard for cloud-native product development
Result: - 10 weeks to production-ready application in client infrastructure, $1.3M saved in lost productivity, zero production defects over a year of delivery. One attorney who had been planning to quit decided to stay because the software transformed their workflow
- Methodology became standard across all 15+ Fortune 500 CIC engagements, with clients specifically requesting Discovery & Framing for new projects. Other Accenture practices adopted it beyond CIC
- Clients implemented the approach internally after CIC engagements ended, continuing to apply structured Discovery & Framing on subsequent products without CIC involvement
public_tags: Backlog Prioritization, Collaborative Design, Customer Journey Mapping, De-Risking, Discovery & Framing, Enterprise Product Development, Framing, Hypothesis-Driven Development, Lean Product Management, MVP Definition, OKR Definition, Product Validation, ROI demonstration, Risk Management, Shaping, Stakeholder Alignment, Stakeholder alignment, Stakeholder management, User Research, agile methodology, agile transformation, backlog prioritization, capability building, change management, cloud-native development, consensus-building, cross-functional collaboration, extreme programming, hypothesis-driven development, hypothesis-driven validation, knowledge transfer, lean product strategy, measurable business outcomes, organizational politics, product-market fit, rapid prototyping, scenario-based planning, strategic alignment, strategic product decisions, technical feasibility assessment, transparent collaboration, trust-building, user-centered design
Purpose: Establish Discovery & Framing as core CIC practice that systematically de-risked product development by validating user needs, technical feasibility, and business value before significant investment, enabling clients to invest confidently in the right solutions.
Performance: - Clients specifically requested methodology — practice adopted across 15+ Fortune 500 engagements
- Applied across diverse contexts — Kaiser condition management, American Express digital experiences, pharma, banking — adapted to each client
- JP Morgan legal: 10 weeks to production, $1.3M saved in lost productivity, zero production defects over a year, attorney retention impact
- Methodology documentation became Accenture standard adopted beyond CIC
- Clients continued Discovery & Framing independently after CIC engagements ended
Process: - Codified 4-8 week two-phase approach: Discovery (understanding problem space through current state review, stakeholder and user interviews, technology deep dives, persona development, user journeys, culminating in scenario stories grounded in user emotion and JTBD) and Framing (defining solution through journey mapping, feature shaping and prioritization, success metrics, rapid prototyping with lo-fi mockups, tight test-and-iterate loops, and value vs. implementation matrix — culminating in prioritized backlog delivering day-one value)
- Built reusable templates and facilitation guides for each phase enabling consistent application
- Trained 150+ CIC practitioners in collaborative two-phase methodology emphasizing different purposes and activities
- Applied across JP Morgan (legal case management), Kaiser (condition management), American Express (digital experiences), global pharmaceutical leader (plasma donors), and other Fortune 500 contexts
- Built confidence through transparent collaboration — invited stakeholders into Discovery activities, collaboratively defined solution in Framing
- Established quality gates ensuring both Discovery and Framing completion before development investment
5PSummary: JP Morgan's legal team had spent two years trying to solve a case management problem and came to the CIC confident they already knew the solution. User research with the actual attorneys revealed the underlying problem was different from what had been assumed — and we redirected the engagement. This experience drove me to formalize Discovery & Framing as a repeatable methodology across the CIC. Training 150+ practitioners and applying it across JP Morgan, Kaiser, American Express, and other Fortune 500 clients, the methodology became Accenture's standard for cloud-native product development — with clients continuing the practice independently after engagements ended.

Title: Driving Agile Adoption for JP Morgan's Identity & Access Management Transformation
Use Case(s): Drive Jira adoption in teams where the tool exists but isn't being used effectively. Introduce agile practices to resolve fragmented workflows and delayed feedback loops across departments.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you facilitated Agile onboarding for JP Morgan's Identity & Access Management transformation and the impact it had on cross-functional team coordination?, How did you configure and integrate Jira to address the inefficiencies and fragmented workflows during JP Morgan's I&AM transformation?, Describe the challenges you faced with resistance to Agile practices at JP Morgan and how you overcame them to improve collaboration and streamline workflows?, Walk me through the process of enhancing delivery tracking and reporting transparency for JP Morgan's I&AM team using Jira?, What specific training and development strategies did you implement to accelerate Jira adoption and improve workflow optimization at JP Morgan?
Situation: Following the 2014 data breach, JP Morgan's I&AM Uplift Program required 341 CIB SOX/SSAE16 applications to implement new security controls on an aggressive timeline. Tracking remediation progress and coordinating work across hundreds of application teams required centralized tooling. While Jira was the default Agile tool for the organization, the team had not fully adopted its functionality — leading to fragmented workflows, limited visibility into remediation status, and difficulty identifying which applications were falling behind.
Task: I was tasked with configuring Jira to meet the specific needs of the I&AM program and ensuring its successful adoption by the team. This included tailoring Jira workflows to manage epics, user stories, and sprints effectively, while driving alignment on Agile practices to enhance collaboration and improve delivery tracking.
Action: - Configured Jira workflows to align with the team's needs, incorporating epics, user stories, and sprints to streamline task tracking and ensure better visibility across workstreams
- Integrated Jira with existing tools and systems to create a successful project management environment, reducing friction in workflows
- Led Agile onboarding sessions, focusing on the value of iterative development and collaborative problem-solving, to address resistance and build buy-in for both Jira and Agile practices
- Provided training and created user guides tailored to team roles, ensuring ease of use and consistent adoption across departments
- Ran daily stand-ups and sprint planning sessions using Jira to demonstrate its impact on improving team coordination and delivery tracking
Result: The team successfully adopted Jira, leading to improved collaboration, streamlined workflows, and enhanced delivery tracking.
- Cross-functional teams aligned more effectively on priorities, reducing delays and communication gaps
- Progress tracking and reporting became more transparent, enabling stakeholders to monitor delivery timelines and identify potential risks early
- Training and tailored support helped the team adopt Jira quickly, establishing it as the central tool for Agile workflows
- Agile practices combined with Jira's functionality resulted in smoother delivery cycles, boosting stakeholder confidence in the program's execution

Title: Leading RBAC Remediation and Application Team Enablement for JP Morgan's I&AM Uplift
Use Case(s): Provide migration guidance and drive adoption where application teams lack clarity on I&AM platform requirements and RBAC policies. Enable 341 application teams to self-remediate through tailored documentation, functional specifications, and direct enablement rather than centralized enforcement.
Interview Questions: Walk me through how you approached driving adoption of new security requirements across 341 applications when the teams had no advance notice or additional resources?, How did you handle the variety of remediation paths across different technology stacks and application risk profiles?, Tell me about the tiger team model — how did you structure augmentation support for application teams that couldn't absorb the work alone?, How did you handle the transition when Accenture's engagement was cut short?, What did you learn about driving adoption of unplanned mandates across teams with competing priorities?
Situation: In 2014, JP Morgan Chase suffered one of the largest data breaches in U.S. banking history — attackers obtained employee credentials and accessed a corporate server that lacked multi-factor authentication, then moved laterally across 90+ servers with root-level access. The firm responded with a massive Corporate I&AM Uplift Program, doubling its cybersecurity budget and mandating new security controls across the enterprise. On the CIB side, 341 SOX/SSAE16 applications needed to implement new I&AM requirements — RBAC policies, access certification, privileged access controls, user lifecycle management. The problem was this initiative came out of nowhere for the application teams. They had to absorb significant security remediation work on top of their existing delivery commitments and competing priorities, with no additional staffing or budget planned for it.
Task: Lead the engagement between Accenture's team and the CIB application teams to drive adoption of the new I&AM controls — evangelizing the initiative's objectives, helping teams understand the impact to their specific applications, and providing hands-on support to enable remediation as quickly as possible.
Action: - Reached out to application owners and business owners for each of the 341 CIB apps to evangelize the uplift objectives and explain what was required of them
- Taught teams on the fundamentals of RBAC, access certification, and security practices — many app teams had no prior exposure to these disciplines and needed education before they could act
- Led application-by-application analysis with each team to assess their current state, understand the specific impact of the new I&AM requirements, and define a remediation path forward

- Coordinated "tiger teams" of developers available to augment application teams and help implement the security solutions — critical because app teams couldn't absorb the work alone alongside their existing commitments
- Navigated a variety of remediation paths and exception processes depending on each application's technology stack, sunset dates, risk profile, and feasibility — not every app got the same treatment
- Worked with line-of-business RBAC leads and compliance stakeholders to ensure remediation plans aligned with SOX/SSAE16 requirements
Result: - Application teams moved from zero awareness of the I&AM uplift requirements to active remediation planning and execution across the 341-app portfolio
- Tiger team model enabled application teams to make progress on security remediation without derailing their existing delivery commitments
- Remediation paths tailored to each application's context — technology, risk, timeline — rather than forcing a one-size-fits-all approach
- Accenture engagement was cut short due to funding constraints, requiring structured handoff of in-progress work and remediation plans to JP Morgan's internal teams to continue execution

Purpose: Ensure application teams understand the functional and technical impacts of migrating to the new I&AM platform and to document the changes effectively.
Performance: - I&AM uplift awareness and remediation planning driven across 341 CIB SOX/SSAE16 applications
- Tiger team augmentation model enabled parallel security remediation without disrupting existing delivery
- Tailored remediation paths accounted for technology, risk, and sunset timelines — no one-size-fits-all approach
Process: - Organized workshops with stakeholders and technical teams to analyze and document migration impacts
- Created functional specifications and technical documentation to ensure application teams aligned with RBAC policies and privacy standards, such as GDPR
- Identified and addressed security risks associated with unauthorized access, ensuring compliance throughout the process
5PSummary: Following the 2014 data breach, JP Morgan launched a firm-wide I&AM Uplift requiring 341 CIB SOX/SSAE16 applications to implement new security controls — but the initiative landed on application teams with no additional budget or staffing. I led outreach to application and business owners, educated teams on RBAC and security fundamentals, ran app-by-app impact analysis, and coordinated tiger teams to augment remediation. This moved the portfolio from zero awareness to active remediation planning, with tailored paths for each application's technology and risk profile.

Title: Driving Cloud-Native Innovation through TICARA Framework
Use Case(s): Eliminate internal bottlenecks where AWS provisioning and DevOps setup slow innovation timelines. Build reusable cloud-native frameworks enabling teams to deliver prototypes in hours instead of days.
Interview Questions: Tell me about creating the TICARA framework to address AWS provisioning and DevOps inefficiencies at Accenture's Liquid Studio?, How did you leverage cloud-native architecture and platform engineering to accelerate innovation cycles at Accenture?, Describe the process of implementing Infrastructure as Code within the TICARA framework to streamline AWS account setup and billing management?, Walk me through how you embedded DevOps best practices into the TICARA framework to enable rapid prototyping at Accenture's Liquid Studio?, How did the TICARA framework impact the time-to-market for proof-of-concept applications at Accenture, and what were the measurable outcomes?

Title: Empowering Teams through Decentralized Decision-Making
Use Case(s): Decentralize decision-making in teams where centralized structures create bottlenecks and stifle innovation. Build autonomous teams with clear accountability and agile feedback loops to accelerate responsiveness.

These constraints limited the teams' ability to respond swiftly to immediate challenges and opportunities, reducing their effectiveness and morale.

- Adopted a servant leadership model, where leaders acted as facilitators rather than decision-makers, guiding teams toward solutions while allowing them to take ownership of decisions. Anchors or team leads focused on removing obstacles rather than controlling outcomes
- Embedded agile and lean practices such as iterative development, user-centered design, and continuous retrospectives, enabling teams to adjust strategies based on real-time feedback and user needs
- Created clear, flexible guidelines for decentralized decision-making aligned with overarching goals, ensuring teams had the autonomy to innovate while staying within project objectives

- Cultivated an environment of psychological safety where team members were encouraged to share ideas and challenge assumptions without fear of judgment, mirroring the cultural principles that drive self-managed teams
Result: - Decentralized decision-making removed bottlenecks, allowing teams to act quickly and efficiently
- Teams delivered high-quality, innovative solutions that aligned more closely with client needs
- Empowered teams reported higher engagement and satisfaction, contributing to improved retention and long-term project stability
- Teams achieved stronger cohesion and accountability, consistently meeting project milestones and delivering impactful outcomes

Title: Enabling New Ways of Working Through Immersive Coaching and Transformation Labs
Use Case(s): Assess workforce readiness against target competencies in product management, agile, DevOps, and modern engineering. Build competency frameworks and transformation roadmaps that diagnose gaps and prescribe targeted interventions — dojo, lab, immersive, or self-paced — then measure progress to iterate.
Interview Questions: Tell me about building competency frameworks to assess and close skill gaps across product management agile and DevOps?, How did you diagnose individual and team gaps and prescribe the right mix of coaching interventions?, Describe how you used brain-based learning techniques to make capability development stick?, Walk me through how you measured progress and iterated on coaching activities based on the data?, How did the competency frameworks scale across CloudFirst as a standard diagnostic tool?
Situation: Accenture teams needed to shift from traditional IT delivery to product-centric ways of working — but the gap between where people were and where they needed to be varied widely across product management, agile, DevOps, Python, and modern engineering. Without a structured way to assess readiness, map gaps, and prescribe the right interventions, coaching efforts were inconsistent and hard to scale. This work had to happen alongside active client delivery — coaching people both on the bench and while staffed on projects.
Task: Build a hands-on capability development program — from competency frameworks and gap assessments through custom training material, coaching delivery (dojo, lab, immersive, self-paced), and progress measurement — enabling scalable, measurable transformation to product-centric ways of working while maintaining active client delivery.
Action: - Defined target-state competency models across product management, agile, DevOps, cloud native practices, modern engineering, and related disciplines
- Assessed individuals and teams against target competencies to establish baselines and identify gaps
- Created transformation roadmaps mapping current state to target state with prescribed interventions based on gap severity and learning style
- Developed training material and learning content tailored to assessed gaps — not off-the-shelf, built for the specific needs identified
- Identified and triaged individuals for coaching based on assessments — matching people to the right intervention whether they were on the bench or actively staffed on client projects
- dojo (1:many), lab (1:1), immersive training, and self-paced learning based on individual and group needs
- Applied brain-based learning techniques — connecting new concepts to prior experience, bite-sized modules, iterative practice with real-world challenges
- Measured progress individually and as groups, iterating on coaching activities based on what the data showed
- Applied "I do, we do, you do" coaching progression with live coding scenarios on real-world challenges
- Embedded reflective retrospectives to assess progress and promote continuous improvement
- Managed all of this concurrently with active CIC client delivery responsibilities
Result: - Teams transitioned from traditional IT delivery to product-centric ways of working, closing assessed competency gaps across product management, agile, DevOps, and modern engineering
- Competency frameworks became the standard diagnostic tool for prescribing coaching interventions across CloudFirst
- Clear career pathways and capability roadmaps enabled employees to grow in sync with evolving client needs
- Retention rates improved as team members felt supported in their growth and career progression

Title: Escaping the Velocity Trap for Sustainable Delivery
Use Case(s): Recover teams trapped in velocity-driven cultures where story points are treated as performance measures. Replace vanity metrics with actionable scorecards in environments where output measures misalign with business goals and drive gaming, burnout, and local optimization.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you shifted teams away from sprint velocity as a performance metric?, How did you design scorecards around actionable delivery and team-health metrics to replace velocity-based tracking?, Describe your approach to educating stakeholders on why velocity is not a KPI and how you shifted them to outcome-based thinking?, Walk me through how integrating team well-being into delivery health indicators improved retention and sustainable performance?, How did you build a continuous improvement culture focused on learning and experimentation rather than hitting velocity targets?
Situation: Many technology teams — both at Accenture and at clients — treated sprint velocity as a performance metric, but velocity is a gauge, not a control. Story points are judgments, not measurable quantities — you can't derive a quantitative measure from qualitative input. Teams optimizing for velocity gamed the number, pushed out low-value work to hit targets, and burned out chasing a metric that told them nothing about the value of what they were producing. The result was higher defect rates, misalignment with user needs, and demoralized teams spending more time meeting estimates than solving problems.
Task: Shift teams away from velocity as a performance measure toward scorecards built around actionable, product-centric, and team-health metrics — while maintaining delivery predictability and improving alignment with business outcomes
Action: - Moved away from velocity as a performance measure — recognizing that story points are judgments, not measurements, and that velocity is a gauge you observe, not a control you optimize. Replaced it with scorecards prioritizing actionable, product-centric, and team-health metrics: lead time, cycle time, throughput, validated hypotheses, backlog health, customer conversation frequency, process improvements over time, and team stability. Continuously refined scorecards as some metrics proved to add no value

- Focused on defect rates, rework frequency, and delivery cadence as key indicators — ensuring speed didn't compromise quality. Rejected planned-to-completed work ratios as meaningful, since the discrepancy between planned and delivered reflects learning, not failure
- Conducted regular well-being check-ins, retrospectives, and team stability tracking — treating turnover, satisfaction, and sustainable pace as first-class delivery indicators, not afterthoughts

- Held workshops explaining why velocity is not a KPI — it doesn't compare across teams (different point scales), it's not actionable (tells you nothing about how to improve), and it drives local optimization when what matters is end-to-end flow. Shifted stakeholders to outcome-based thinking

- Rather than measuring teams against targets, focused on the quality of the improvement culture itself — number of process experiments, institutional changes driven by experiments, and learning velocity. Productivity follows improvement culture, not the other way around
Result: - Defect rates dropped as teams delivered features that consistently aligned with user and business goals
- Stakeholders gained a better understanding of outcome-driven metrics, improving satisfaction and alignment
- Burnout rates decreased, team engagement improved, and retention rates increased
- Other Accenture teams began adopting these practices, validating the CIC's leadership role
- Teams achieved predictable delivery timelines without sacrificing quality, creating a repeatable model for long-term product delivery
public_tags: Agile Leadership, Agile methodologies, Balanced Workflows, Business Alignment, Developer Experience, Modern Engineering Practices, Outcome-Driven Engineering, Productivity Optimization, Psychological Safety, Quality Indicators, Stakeholder alignment, Stakeholder management, Sustainable Development, Team Well-being, User Satisfaction, Value-Based Metrics, agile leadership, agile methodologies, agile planning, business alignment, change management, coaching, continuous improvement culture, cross-functional collaboration, defect reduction, engineering leadership, engineering practices, influencing without authority, modern engineering practices, outcome-based metrics, product impact measurement, psychological safety, quality assurance, quality-centric engineering, stakeholder alignment, stakeholder education, stakeholder management, sustainable delivery, sustainable development, team dynamics, team engagement, team well-being, trust-building, user satisfaction metrics, value-driven delivery
Person: My cross-functional teams at Accenture CIC
Purpose: Shift teams away from velocity-as-KPI toward actionable scorecards measuring delivery health, product value, and team well-being
Performance: - Defect rates dropped through focus on value over velocity
- Stakeholder satisfaction increased through outcome-driven metrics
- Team morale and retention improved as burnout declined
- CIC recognized as model for modern engineering
- Achieved balanced and sustainable delivery
Process: - Redefined success metrics around lead time, cycle time, throughput, and team stability
- Prioritized quality and predictability over velocity targets
- Integrated team well-being into delivery health indicators
- Educated stakeholders on why velocity is a gauge, not a control
- Built continuous improvement culture focused on experiments and learning
5PSummary: Teams treated sprint velocity as a performance metric — but velocity is a gauge, not a control, and story points are judgments, not measurable quantities. Optimizing for velocity led to gaming, burnout, higher defect rates, and misalignment with user needs. I shifted teams to scorecards built around actionable metrics — lead time, cycle time, throughput, validated hypotheses, and team stability — while continuously refining as some metrics proved to add no value. Defect rates dropped, stakeholder satisfaction improved, and morale recovered.

Title: Establishing a ‘Permit to Fail’ Culture for Continuous Improvement
Use Case(s): Transform risk-averse teams where fear of blame causes stagnation and missed innovation opportunities. Build "permit to fail" cultures through transparent post-mortems and structured experimentation.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you established a 'permit to fail' culture at Accenture?, How did you utilize post-mortem practices to reduce error repetition?, Describe implementing safe experimentation and fail-fast culture to enhance project agility?, Walk me through overcoming the team's fear of blame and promoting psychological safety?, How did you enable innovation through structured experimentation and learning-focused leadership?
Situation: The team had a history of avoiding risks due to fear of blame, which led to stagnation, reduced innovation, and slower delivery cycles. This reluctance to experiment caused missed opportunities in adopting modern engineering practices and improving team agility.
Task: Promote a "permit to fail" mindset, where failures are viewed as learning opportunities that contribute to continuous improvement
Action: - Organized non-punitive post-mortems to analyze mistakes and identify lessons learned, encouraging transparent sharing of errors
- Emphasized the value of lessons learned from mistakes, rather than pointing fingers, in team meetings and communications
- Supported lean, low-stakes experiments to test hypotheses, fostering an environment where teams felt comfortable exploring new approach through structured hypotheses and controlled iterations
Result: - With a safety net for failures, team members began experimenting with new technologies and approaches, generating more innovative ideas
- Post-mortem learnings applied to subsequent sprints, preventing recurrence of identified failure patterns and improving overall reliability
- Lean experiments and a fail-fast mindset enabled quicker pivots and shorter project timelines

Title: Fast-Tracking Client Innovation with Emerging Technologies
Use Case(s): Help clients explore AI, blockchain, and emerging technologies when they lack strategies for adoption. Deliver rapid prototypes validating high-impact use cases in days rather than months.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how Accenture's Liquid Studio facilitated rapid prototyping and validation for clients exploring AI, blockchain, and the metaverse?, How did you use design thinking workshops at Accenture to identify high-impact use cases for emerging technologies across industries?, Describe the process you led at Accenture's Liquid Studio to accelerate validation cycles and enable clients to make data-driven innovation decisions?, Walk me through the methods used at Accenture to integrate disruptive technologies like AI and blockchain, resulting in sustained market advantage for clients?, How did Accenture's Liquid Studio position itself as a strategic innovation partner through the use of Lean & DevOps enablement and cross-functional collaboration?
Situation: Clients were seeking innovative solutions to stay competitive in a rapidly evolving market. Many were interested in understanding how disruptive technologies like AI, blockchain, and the metaverse could reshape their business models but lacked clear strategies for adoption and execution. Accenture's Liquid Studio was tasked with helping clients explore and implement emerging technologies, enabling them to accelerate digital transformation and sustain competitive advantage.
Task: I was tasked with leading design thinking workshops, identifying high-impact use cases for disruptive technologies, and ensuring the feasibility and scalability of solutions through rapid prototyping and DevOps automation. The goal was to deliver practical innovation, allowing clients to experiment with emerging technologies in a low-risk, high-reward environment.
Action: - Conducted design thinking workshops with cross-functional teams and clients, brainstorming solutions aligned with their strategic goals
- Identify relevant use cases for technologies like AI, blockchain, and the metaverse and present them in a way that demonstrated value to clients
- Conducted exploratory sessions with clients, mapping potential applications of disruptive technologies to their strategic goals
- Developed working prototypes to showcase real-world applications
- Partnered with industry experts and internal teams to ensure feasibility and scalability
- Implemented continuous integration (CI) pipelines to streamline code testing and deployment
Result: - Clients left with working prototypes or MVPs — not strategy decks — giving them tangible artifacts to drive internal investment decisions

- Liquid Studio's bench depth in modern engineering (cloud-native, JS frameworks, Python, Node) combined with Accenture's broader network (Labs, startup alliance, domain SMEs) meant clients never hit a "we can't prototype that" wall
- Validation cycles compressed from months to weeks, letting clients kill bad ideas cheaply or greenlight good ones with evidence

Title: Solving Ambiguous Problems by Finding Root Causes, Not Treating Symptoms
Use Case(s): Answer interview questions about problem-solving methodology and handling ambiguity. Demonstrate structured approaches to complex, poorly-defined challenges across industries.
Interview Questions: How do you approach solving complex, ambiguous problems at Accenture, especially in cross-industry contexts like financial services and telecommunications?, What is your methodology for uncovering the real problem through hypothesis-driven thinking and validation at Accenture's Cloud Innovation Center (CIC)?, Walk me through a situation where you brought structure to chaos in a politically complex environment at Accenture, ensuring stakeholder alignment and solution design?, How do you enable teams to own execution and solve root causes rather than symptoms in industries such as healthcare and transportation at Accenture?, What is your approach to change leadership when organizations are not initially ready for change, as experienced in your role at Accenture?
Situation: Throughout my career, I've been drawn to the messiest problems - the ones where the real issue isn't obvious, stakeholders are misaligned, and the organization may not even be ready for change. I've learned that most failed initiatives don't fail in execution. They fail because people are solving the wrong problem.
Task: My task, over and over, has been to help teams and organizations step back, find the real problem, and build a path forward that people can actually follow - especially in environments where ambiguity is high and resistance to change is real.
Action: - Get to know the people first, then the problem. Understand the business context, the impacts, the politics. Listen more than I talk
- Bring a broader group together. Look at technology, product, business, and end-to-end impacts. Define a hypothesis - what do we think we're actually solving?
- Prove or disprove the hypothesis. Fail fast if needed. Define a solution design and path forward. Build the roadmap, backlog, and plan. Enable the team to own it
- Execute the plan with regular checkpoints, visible tradeoffs, and continuous alignment
Result: This approach has worked across industries:  financial services, healthcare, telecommunications, transportation. It's helped teams stop spinning on symptoms and start solving root causes. It's brought structure to chaos without killing creativity. And it's helped organizations that weren't ready for change become ready - because they understood the "why" before the "what."
public_tags: Stakeholder alignment, Stakeholder management, ambiguity, ambiguity resolution, capability building, change leadership, change management, chaos to clarity, complexity, continuous alignment, cross-functional collaboration, discovery, framing, how do you approach problems, hypothesis-driven problem solving, hypothesis-driven thinking, industry transformation, methodology, navigating resistance, organizational politics, outcome ownership, problem framing, problem-solving, roadmap development, root cause analysis, solution design, stakeholder alignment, structured problem solving, structured thinking, team dynamics, team empowerment, transformation leadership, trust-building
Purpose: Bring calm, clarity, and structure to complex or ambiguous situations - helping teams solve the right problem, not just the obvious one
Performance: - Repeatable methodology applied across industries
- Reduced rework by aligning on root causes early
- Enabled change in resistant organizations
- Built teams that could sustain progress after I left
Process: - Start with people, not just the problem
- Define hypotheses before jumping to solutions
- Fail fast on wrong paths
- Enable teams to own the outcome
5PSummary: I'm drawn to the messiest problems - poorly defined, politically complex, in organizations that aren't always ready for change. My approach is consistent: start with the people, uncover the real problem through hypothesis and validation, frame a path forward, and enable teams to own execution. This methodology has brought structure to chaos across financial services, healthcare, telecom, and transportation - helping organizations solve the right problems, not just the obvious ones

Title: Implementing BDD for System Behavior Specification
Use Case(s): Specify system behavior across API, business logic, and UI layers using executable Gherkin scenarios. Ensure desired behavior is built correctly and protected from regression.
Interview Questions: How do you use BDD to specify system behavior across multiple layers?, Tell me about implementing test automation for a complex stateful application., What's the difference between BDD and traditional unit testing?, How do you decide what level of test coverage is appropriate?
Situation: MattGPT had grown into a multi-layered application: RAG API handling retrieval and response generation, business logic managing filters and state, and a Streamlit UI with 11+ interactive widgets. Changes in one layer could affect behavior in others — a retrieval tweak might break expected search results, a state management fix might break deeplinks, a UI change might break filter persistence. I needed a way to specify the expected behavior of the entire system, not just individual components, and verify that behavior automatically across all layers. Traditional unit tests couldn't capture cross-layer interactions or express requirements in business-readable language.
Task: Implement a BDD framework that could specify and verify system behavior across API, business logic, and UI layers. Create executable specifications that served both as documentation of expected behavior and as automated regression tests. Establish BDD as the quality gate for any changes affecting user-facing behavior.
Action: - Selected pytest-bdd and Playwright as the testing stack — Gherkin for readable specifications, Playwright for browser automation, pytest for execution
- Given the user is on Explore Stories, When they search for payments, Then results should include JP Morgan stories

- search (4 scenarios), filters (10 scenarios), view switching (6 scenarios), story detail (3 scenarios), deeplinks (4 scenarios), navigation (4 scenarios), pagination (5 scenarios), edge cases (3 scenarios)
- Implemented step definitions that test at the appropriate layer — some scenarios test API responses directly, others test full UI flows
- Built reusable fixtures for browser setup, app URL configuration, and state reset between scenarios
- "Navigation to Explore Stories always starts fresh" with one exception for deeplinks
- Iteratively refined selectors and waits to handle Streamlit's rerun model and dynamic DOM
- Removed scenarios that specified features that didn't exist — tests should document actual behavior, not wishlist items
Result: - 43 BDD scenarios passing across all capability areas
- Executable specifications that serve as living documentation of system behavior
- Regression protection for search, filters, navigation, deeplinks, and state management
- Clear behavioral contracts that can be read by non-technical stakeholders
- Confidence to refactor knowing that breaking changes will be caught immediately
- specify behavior in Gherkin first, then implement or fix
public_tags: API Testing, Behavior-Driven Development, Behavioral Contracts, Cross-Layer Testing, Executable Specifications, Gherkin, Playwright, Regression Protection, Requirements Documentation, Stakeholder Alignment, State Management Testing, Streamlit, Test Automation, UI Testing, pytest-bdd
Person: Engineers implementing test automation, technical leaders establishing quality practices
Purpose: Demonstrate BDD as a discipline for specifying and verifying system behavior across all layers — not just UI testing, but executable specifications that ensure the desired system is actually built
Performance: - 43 BDD scenarios passing
- Coverage across API, business logic, and UI layers
- Zero unspecified behaviors in critical user flows
- Regression protection for 11+ interactive components
Process: - Write Gherkin scenarios specifying expected behavior before implementing changes
- Organize scenarios by capability, not by layer
- Implement step definitions at the appropriate layer (API, logic, or UI)
- Run full suite after any change affecting user-facing behavior
- Remove or update scenarios when requirements change
- Treat feature files as living documentation
5PSummary: MattGPT spans multiple layers — RAG API, business logic, and Streamlit UI — where changes in one layer can affect behavior in others. I implemented BDD using pytest-bdd and Playwright to specify and verify system behavior across all layers. Wrote 43 Gherkin scenarios covering search, filters, navigation, deeplinks, and state management, organized by capability rather than implementation layer. The feature files serve as both executable specifications and living documentation. BDD ensures the desired system behavior is actually built and protected from regression — the same discipline as eval-driven development for RAG, applied to the full application stack.

Title: Implementing Eval-Driven Development for RAG Systems
Use Case(s): Establish quality gates for RAG systems where traditional unit tests don't apply. Make confident architectural decisions by measuring retrieval and response quality against real-world queries.
Interview Questions: How do you test RAG systems where traditional unit tests don't apply?, Tell me about a time you used data to make an architectural decision., How do you prevent regressions when changing LLM prompts or retrieval logic?, What's your approach to quality engineering for AI/ML systems?
Situation: Traditional software testing doesn't translate directly to RAG systems. Unit tests can verify that code executes, but they can't tell you whether the system retrieves the right stories for a given question or whether the LLM response actually answers what the user asked. Early in MattGPT development, I was making changes to prompts, retrieval logic, and confidence thresholds based on intuition and spot-checking a few queries. Sometimes changes that seemed like improvements broke other cases I hadn't thought to test. I needed a systematic way to measure quality across the full range of questions users might ask — and a way to know immediately when a change caused regression.
Task: Build an evaluation framework that could measure RAG system quality across retrieval relevance, response accuracy, and edge case handling. Use the eval suite as the primary decision-making tool for all architectural changes — no changes without running the suite, no merges if the pass rate drops.
Action: - synthesis (themes, patterns across stories), surgical (specific projects, clients), and edge cases (ambiguous, off-topic, adversarial)
- Built a golden query suite with 61 real questions representing what recruiters and hiring managers actually ask
- which stories should be retrieved, what the response should include, what it should not hallucinate
- Created automated eval runner that executes queries against the live system and scores pass/fail based on defined criteria
- Established baseline metrics before making any architectural changes
- entity gate removal, confidence threshold tuning, prompt rewrites, retrieval parameter adjustments
- Added new golden queries whenever I discovered an edge case in production
- Documented eval-driven development as a core practice in the codebase
Result: - 61 golden queries covering synthesis, surgical, and edge case categories
- 98% pass rate maintained across multiple architectural changes
- Enabled confident removal of entity gate — eval proved simplification didn't hurt quality
- Caught prompt regressions before they reached production
- Eval suite became the source of truth for "does this change improve the system?"
- define expected behavior, measure, change, measure again
public_tags: Architectural Decision-Making, Automated Evaluation, Change Management, Cross-Functional Collaboration, Edge Case Handling, Eval-Driven Development, Hypothesis-Driven Development, LLM Quality Assurance, Prompt Engineering, Quality Gates, RAG Architecture, Regression Prevention, Semantic Search Evaluation, Stakeholder Alignment, Test Automation
Person: Engineers building RAG/LLM systems, technical leaders evaluating AI quality practices
Purpose: Demonstrate how to apply rigorous quality engineering to RAG systems where traditional testing approaches fall short, using eval suites as the primary tool for confident decision-making
Performance: - 61 golden queries across three categories
- 98% eval pass rate maintained
- Zero regressions merged to production
- Eval suite used for all major architectural decisions
Process: - Categorize query types (synthesis, surgical, edge cases)
- Define expected behavior for each golden query
- Automate eval execution and scoring
- Establish baseline before changes
- Run suite after every significant change
- Add new queries when edge cases surface
5PSummary: Traditional testing doesn't translate directly to RAG systems — unit tests can't tell you if the right stories were retrieved or if the response actually answers the question. I built a 61-query eval suite covering synthesis questions, surgical queries, and edge cases, with defined expected behavior for each. The suite became the decision-making tool for all architectural changes: entity gate removal, confidence tuning, prompt rewrites. Eval pass rate held at 98% across multiple changes, and regressions were caught before reaching production. Eval-driven development is to RAG what TDD is to traditional software — define expected behavior first, then build with confidence.

Title: Implementing Responsible AI Governance for Patient Data Privacy and Compliance at Fortune 500 healthcare company
Use Case(s): Implement responsible AI governance where patient data privacy concerns block AI adoption in healthcare. Build HIPAA-compliant frameworks with anonymization, encryption, and auditability when executive stakeholders fear regulatory and reputational risk.
Interview Questions: Tell me about implementing responsible AI governance to ensure HIPAA compliance and patient data privacy at Major U.S. Health System?, How did you mitigate data security risks while maintaining 100% HIPAA compliance during the AI initiative at a Major U.S. Health System?, Describe the process of establishing a privacy-preserving architecture and transparent governance for AI in chronic disease management at a Major U.S. Health System?, Walk me through the cross-functional execution and collaboration efforts that led to increased clinician and patient adoption of AI-powered tools at Major U.S. Health System?, How did you manage executive stakeholder communication to strengthen trust and consider broader implementation of AI initiatives at Major U.S. Health System?
Situation: As part of the organization's initiative to leverage AI in chronic disease management, there was an urgent need to ensure HIPAA compliance and safeguard patient data privacy. The CIO expressed significant concern about the risks associated with processing sensitive medical information, as any mishandling could lead to regulatory fines, reputational damage, or loss of stakeholder trust.
Task: Develop a responsible AI governance framework that ensured data privacy, addressed HIPAA compliance, and built trust among stakeholders. The framework needed to include policies, risk management practices, and transparent auditing to support secure, ethical AI development.
Action: - Brought together representatives from legal, compliance, IT security, data science, and clinical departments to align on responsible AI practices and HIPAA compliance
- Integrated data anonymization, encryption, and differential privacy into AI models to protect sensitive information at all stages of processing
- Created clear policies for data access, model transparency, and accountability. Set up continuous monitoring and auditing systems to track performance and identify risks

- Regularly updated the CIO and executive team during a pilot phase, providing reporting and adjustments based on feedback. Trained staff and raised awareness of privacy practices to build confidence across teams
- Operationalized Accenture's Responsible AI Framework — previously principles-only — into working governance with anonymization, encryption, differential privacy, and HIPAA-compliant data pipelines
Result: - Achieved full HIPAA compliance throughout the pilot, with continuous monitoring and auditing in place
- Built cross-functional governance structure (legal, compliance, IT security, data science, clinical) that provided a repeatable model for future AI initiatives
- Executive team approved consideration of broader AI implementation based on the governance framework's performance during the pilot

Purpose: Ensure HIPAA compliance and protect patient privacy while enabling scalable, responsible use of AI for chronic disease management
Performance: - Achieved 100% HIPAA compliance during the pilot
- Increased clinical exposure to AI-powered tools for diabetes and condition management
Process: - Designed and implemented a responsible AI governance framework aligned with enterprise data ethics
- Formed and led a cross-functional governance team (compliance, legal, security, data science, clinical)
- Integrated privacy-first techniques (anonymization, encryption, differential privacy) into AI pipelines
- Created policies for access, transparency, and accountability
- Delivered executive briefings and enabled stakeholder alignment through training and pilot oversight
5PSummary: As the healthcare provider adopted AI for chronic disease management, the CIO raised urgent concerns about HIPAA compliance and patient data privacy — mishandling sensitive medical information risked regulatory fines and reputational damage. I led a cross-functional team with industry SMEs to establish privacy-preserving architecture, transparent governance, and operational oversight. This achieved 100% HIPAA compliance and increased clinical exposure to AI-powered tools, building executive confidence for broader implementation.

Title: Integrating Play into Work to Enhance Team Motivation
Use Case(s): Sustain engagement in high-pressure environments where continuous delivery and tight deadlines cause fatigue and burnout. Integrate structured play — competitions, rituals, gamified recognition — to strengthen bonds and maintain morale.
Interview Questions: Tell me about integrating structured play into high-pressure delivery environments to enhance team motivation?, How did you use Chaos Engineering challenges to improve incident response?, Describe the impact of gamified peer recognition on team morale and collaboration?, Walk me through implementing Capture the Flag competitions to foster a collaborative culture?, How did monthly rituals and structured play help sustain engagement across multi-year engagements?
Situation: The Cloud Innovation Center operated in a high-pressure, fast-paced environment, where continuous delivery and tight deadlines placed a heavy cognitive load on teams. Over time, this led to fatigue, lower engagement, and an increase in stress-related burnout, affecting overall team morale and productivity.
Task: Establish structured, engaging, and high-impact team activities that promote collaboration, problem-solving, and stress relief while reinforcing modern engineering and Agile principles.
Action: - Implemented "Chaos Days," simulating unpredictable system failures where teams had to collaboratively troubleshoot real-world problems in high-stakes, time-sensitive scenarios. This transformed traditional failure response exercises into a fun, high-energy learning experience
- Launched "Capture the Flag" security challenges and competitive coding events, boosting team enthusiasm while reinforcing problem-solving, security awareness, and technical acumen

- Organized monthly "Unplug & Recharge" events-offering low-pressure, social team-building activities like creative brainstorming games, hackathons, and informal tech-sharing sessions to foster camaraderie and innovation

- Developed a peer-to-peer learning leaderboard, where team members could earn recognition for mentorship, knowledge sharing, and innovative contributions, reinforcing a collaborative and growth-oriented culture
Result: - Play-based learning exercises transformed workplace morale, increasing enthusiasm, participation, and job satisfaction
- Through Chaos Engineering simulations, teams developed faster, more effective troubleshooting skills, reducing system downtime
- Knowledge-sharing games and gamification increased peer learning, while friendly competitions strengthened cross-functional collaboration
- Structured play embedded in Agile workflows reduced stress levels and improved retention

Title: Keeping Products Alive through Continuous Feedback and Adaptation
Use Case(s): Sustain product momentum post-launch where features drift from user needs and adoption stalls. Embed continuous feedback loops to keep products evolving with real user input.
Interview Questions: Describe the process of implementing continuous feedback loops and adaptive decision-making to improve retention rates for products in a cross-industry setting?, How did you empower client teams to dynamically adapt their products based on real feedback, rather than a launch-and-forget approach?
Situation: Many clients struggled to sustain product momentum post-launch.

Fortune 500 organizations treated product launch as the finish line — teams disbanded, budgets closed, and products went into maintenance mode. When market conditions shifted or user feedback revealed misalignment, there was no mechanism to respond without re-initiating a full project cycle. Internally, delivery teams were incentivized to ship and move on to the next engagement, not to sustain and evolve. Shifting to continuous product evolution required changing how both clients and delivery teams defined "done."

Features often failed to align with evolving user needs, leading to stalled adoption and diminished customer satisfaction. To address these challenges, there was a need to establish a culture of continuous improvement and embed processes for dynamically adapting products to user feedback.
Task: Help clients refine their products for long-term success by embedding feedback-driven processes and aligning feature development with real-world user needs. Drive continuous iteration to ensure product/market fit remains relevant over time.
Action: - Designed iterative loops to monitor feature performance, gather real-time user feedback, and implement data-driven refinements
- Focused on prioritizing high-impact updates that aligned with user needs and business goals while minimizing unnecessary complexity
- Partnered with clients to gather actionable insights from end users, ensuring ongoing alignment with market demands
- Conducted regular assessments to ensure products continued to meet evolving user requirements and business objectives
- Delivered workshops and provided frameworks to enable client teams to independently adopt iterative processes and maintain adaptability
Result: - Clients shifted from "launch and move on" to continuous product evolution — teams kept iterating based on real user data instead of treating delivery as the finish line
- Feature decisions moved from stakeholder assumptions to validated learning — HDD and Build-Measure-Learn loops gave product teams evidence to prioritize, pivot, or kill features post-launch
- Practices sustained after CIC engagement ended — clients internalized feedback loops and continued iterating independently, proving the product mindset took root beyond the consulting engagement

Title: Leadership Philosophy – How I Lead
Use Case(s): Answer interview questions about leadership style, team building, and engineering culture development. Articulate philosophy for empowerment-based leadership in technology organizations.
Interview Questions: What are your core leadership principles and how do they show up day to day?, How do you build trust with a new team in the first 30 days?, What does servant leadership look like in practice when you're running a 150-person organization?, How do you make decisions when the team disagrees and there's no clear right answer?, What's your approach to giving hard feedback without damaging the relationship?
Situation: Across roles ranging from modern engineering initiatives to leading a 150-person Cloud Innovation Center, I learned that successful teams are built on trust, clarity, and shared purpose — not just process or tools.
Task: My task as a leader has always been to create an environment where engineers, designers, and product teams can do the best work of their careers.
Action: My leadership approach blends empathy, clarity, and modern engineering rigor:
- Lead with purpose and make intent explicit
- Build psychological safety so people can take risks and speak honestly
- Push decisions to the people closest to the work
- Use transparency, direct feedback, and open communication
- Ground engineering culture in automation, continuous delivery, quality-first practices
- Coach teams to think clearly, not just execute tasks
Result: This philosophy enabled teams to accelerate delivery 4x, improve quality, strengthen cross-functional trust, and deliver meaningful work with pride and ownership.

Title: Lowering Barriers to Adoption for American Express’s Virtual Payments Platform
Use Case(s): Lower adoption barriers for platforms where missing SDKs, fragmented documentation, and no sandbox block client integration. Build frictionless developer experiences enabling faster onboarding and legacy transitions.

Situation: American Express launched its next-gen Virtual Payments Platform (VPP) to replace a legacy mainframe system. While the new platform offered robust features — like real-time controls, embedded reporting, and flexible APIs — adoption lagged among prestige clients, especially those without mature development teams. The lack of supportive SDKs, fragmented documentation, and no sandbox environment made it difficult for these clients to evaluate and integrate the platform.
Task: Reduce the burden of adoption by creating a frictionless developer and integrator experience — enabling clients to quickly understand the value of VPP, explore its capabilities, and integrate with minimal resources.

- Developed SDKs across multiple tech stacks (Java, .NET, Python, etc.) with clear README files, sample integrations, and "day-one" guides
- Created use case-driven API documentation, emphasizing real-world scenarios
- Built a secure, virtual sandbox environment with refreshed client data to support hands-on exploration and testing
- Integrated CI/CD pipelines to automatically update documentation and APIs in step with backend evolution
Result: - Increased API consumption and trial usage within 3 months of relaunch
- Reduced client onboarding time significantly
- Boosted VPP adoption across prestige clients, accelerating migration away from legacy systems and showcasing platform value

Title: De-Risking RBC’s Corporate Banking Platform Investment
Use Case(s): Assess legacy or vendor platform architecture where performance and scalability limitations constrain business growth — including scenarios where the right recommendation is to stop investing. Deliver strategic roadmaps with gap analysis and heat maps that give leadership evidence-based justification for platform decisions, whether to proceed, pivot, or de-prioritize.

Situation: RBC's existing corporate banking systems were struggling with performance, scalability limitations and lacking digital offerings — legacy architecture constraints were affecting operational efficiency and compliance alignment. RBC was evaluating Bottomline WebSeries as the payment hub engine for its Day2Day Corporate Banking platform to replace the aging RBC Express portal. With several strategic initiatives converging, leadership needed an honest assessment of whether WebSeries could deliver what the business required before committing further investment.
Task: Conduct an architecture assessment and gap/fit analysis to determine whether WebSeries could meet RBC's requirements — drawing on my prior experience implementing the same platform on a large-scale payments program.
Action: - Performed a structured architecture assessment, scoring maturity across Data, Application, Technology, and Business Architecture dimensions against industry reference architectures for financial services
- Conducted code reviews, stakeholder interviews, and documentation analysis of the current-state RBC Express platform
- Evaluated WebSeries capabilities against RBC's requirements, drawing on direct implementation experience to identify where the platform would fall short
- Created architectural heat maps identifying gaps between what RBC needed and what WebSeries could deliver
- Consulted with RBC's enterprise architecture team on solution blueprint, delivering logical and system models for a new target system with transparent cost-to-fix vs. benefit comparison
- Conducted workshops with RBC leadership, aligning business goals with technical findings
- Identified that WebSeries lacked the modularity required for RBC's long-term digital operating model — an honest finding the assessment was designed to surface
Result: - Assessment provided data-driven justification for a strategic pivot — preventing investment in a platform path that couldn't deliver
- Enabled RBC to reallocate critical technical resources toward higher-priority initiatives
- De-risked the technology portfolio by surfacing the gaps before significant spend was committed
- Heat maps and roadmap artifacts remained reference material for future modernization planning

Title: MattGPT: Human-Centered UX Design & Information Architecture
Use Case(s): Design information architecture for discovery-oriented interfaces where traditional portfolio patterns fail. Balance structured exploration with conversational discovery across multiple user personas with different needs.
Interview Questions: Tell me about designing the information architecture and UX system for MattGPT during your sabbatical, focusing on how you balanced structured exploration with natural language conversations?, How did you implement the dual-view interface approach in MattGPT to cater to different user personas like recruiters and hiring managers?, Describe the process of creating the intuitive design specification for MattGPT's React implementation, including the use of the 5P Summary framework?, Walk me through the challenges you faced in designing a conversational discovery pattern for MattGPT's 130+ career stories and how you addressed them using Streamlit and CSS workarounds?, How did you define and utilize user personas in shaping the features of MattGPT, and what impact did this have on the overall user experience?
Situation: With the product vision established for MattGPT, I faced a critical UX challenge: how do you design an interface that makes 130+ career stories feel approachable rather than overwhelming? Traditional portfolio websites rely on chronological timelines or project grids, but neither pattern suited the conversational, discovery-oriented experience I envisioned. The interface needed to support multiple user journeys: recruiters scanning quickly for relevant experience, hiring managers diving deep into specific capabilities, and technical evaluators assessing my design thinking process itself. Additionally, I had to balance two competing needs: enabling structured exploration through filters and categories while also supporting unstructured, natural language conversations about my background. The design needed to feel professional and polished while remaining authentic to my personal brand and working style.
Task: Design and document a complete information architecture and UI/UX system for MattGPT that would: 1) Support both structured exploration ('Explore Stories') and unstructured conversation ('Ask MattGPT') user journeys, 2) Surface the most relevant content quickly while enabling deeper exploration for interested users, 3) Demonstrate my UX design capabilities through the quality of wireframes, interaction patterns, and design documentation, 4) Create a React-based design system with reusable component patterns, and 5) Make the interface intuitive enough that users could navigate without instruction yet sophisticated enough to handle complex filtering and contextual conversations. Additionally, I needed to ship a functional MVP quickly, which meant implementing an approximation of the design vision in Streamlit despite its CSS constraints, while maintaining the full React specification for future migration. I needed to produce professional-grade design artifacts that could be shared in interviews as proof of my human-centered design thinking and attention to user experience.
Action: I began with user journey mapping, defining three primary personas based on how different users would interact with MattGPT: 'The Recruiter' (high-volume screener needing speed, filtering, and quick validation of experience threshold), 'The Hiring Manager' (decision-maker needing depth, narrative structure, and verifiable metrics), and 'The Content User' (myself, needing quick story retrieval for interview preparation and synthesis). For each persona, I mapped their primary needs, feature drivers, and typical journeys through the application. The Recruiter persona drove design of Table View with high data density, Timeline View for career evolution, and advanced filtering by technology and industry. The Hiring Manager persona drove the Detail View with full STAR breakdowns, Key Metrics Sidebar for quantifiable proof, and Related Projects for pattern recognition. The Content User persona drove the Ask MattGPT conversational interface for fast retrieval and synthesis of relevant stories based on interview questions.
Result: The UX design and information architecture work produced several key deliverables: I created a detailed design specification in the mattgpt-design-spec GitHub repository documenting the complete information architecture, wireframes, and component patterns for the React implementation. The dual-view approach (Card View for scanning, Detail View for deep exploration) balanced different user needs, with the 5P Summary framework serving as the hero content element that distills complex STAR narratives into scannable executive summaries. The conversational interface design for Ask MattGPT enables natural language queries like 'Have you worked with global teams?' with contextual answers and AI-suggested follow-up questions. For the Streamlit MVP implementation, I developed a scoped CSS approach using [class*='st-key-topnav_'] selectors that maintains visual polish despite framework constraints - this pattern proved reusable and resilient across Streamlit updates. The information architecture successfully balances structure and flexibility: users can choose structured exploration through filters (domain, competency, technology) or unstructured conversation with MattGPT. The design artifacts in mattgpt-design-spec provide a complete specification that bridges design and engineering, demonstrating both UX thinking and technical implementation considerations in a format that can be shared in interviews.

- 5P Summary framework made STAR narratives scannable at executive level — reducing time-to-insight for portfolio visitors

- Mapped user journeys and identified how each persona shaped specific features

I defined three user personas (Recruiter, Hiring Manager, Content User) and mapped how each shaped specific features, developed the 5P Summary framework as a scannable hero element, and designed dual-view interfaces with conversational discovery patterns. I documented the complete React design system with component patterns, color palette, and typography hierarchy. To deliver a functional MVP quickly, I implemented an approximation in Streamlit using CSS workarounds to fight framework constraints, with the design spec representing the full React vision for future migration.

Title: MattGPT: Product Vision & Strategic Positioning - Transforming Portfolio Differentiation
Use Case(s): Define product vision and strategic positioning for novel portfolio formats in competitive job markets. Demonstrate product thinking through the portfolio product itself, showing technical and strategic capabilities in action.
Interview Questions: Tell me about the development of MattGPT during your sabbatical and how it served as a portfolio differentiator in the Technology & Software industry?, How did you utilize AI/ML, RAG, Python, and semantic search technologies to build MattGPT as an AI-powered portfolio assistant?, Describe the process of creating a NOW/NEXT/LATER roadmap for MattGPT and how it helped in achieving the product vision and strategic positioning?, Walk me through how you established and used OKRs to maintain focus and avoid scope creep while developing MattGPT?, How did the implementation of semantic search across 130+ career stories in MattGPT demonstrate your product thinking and technical capabilities?
Situation: During a sabbatical in early 2025 following 18+ years at Accenture with extensive Fortune 500 transformation experience, I recognized that traditional resumes failed to showcase the depth of my experience or differentiate me for Director/VP-level roles. I also saw an opportunity - by building an AI-powered portfolio assistant, I could simultaneously solve this problem and gain hands-on experience with modern AI/ML, RAG, Python, and semantic search technologies
Task: Define a product vision for an AI-powered portfolio assistant that would: 1) Differentiate me in a competitive job market by showcasing not just what I've done, but how I think and solve problems, 2) Enable recruiters and hiring managers to have natural conversations about my experience rather than reading static documents, 3) Demonstrate my own product thinking and enable me to learn modern AI/ML technologies hands-on through the portfolio itself, and 4) Create a scalable solution that could evolve beyond just a portfolio into an insightful career assistant. I needed to stay focused and avoid over-engineering while establishing clear success metrics, defining the value proposition, creating a product roadmap, and building a compelling business case for investing significant personal time and resources into this initiative.
Action: I positioned MattGPT as a conversational portfolio - moving from passive document to active conversation partner. I created a NOW/NEXT/LATER roadmap: NOW (MVP) focused on core semantic search and filtering; NEXT would enhance search intelligence with hybrid keyword+semantic search and query rewriting; LATER would add job fit matching where users could paste a job description and get tailored responses. To maintain focus, I established OKRs including demonstrating technical capabilities through architecture documentation and code quality, enabling efficient discovery of relevant experience through search, and differentiating myself in the job search process. I created a backlog organized around core user journeys: "Explore Stories" for browsing and filtering, "Ask MattGPT" for conversational discovery, and "About Matt" to tell my story and give an overview of the application. I named the assistant "Agy" after my late Plott Hound, adding personal narrative that humanized the product and demonstrated the importance of storytelling in product design.
Result: The clear product vision and roadmap kept me focused throughout development. I delivered an MVP with semantic search across 130+ stories, avoiding scope creep by "ruthlessly prioritizing" new feature ideas against my core OKRs. The product itself became proof of my capabilities by demonstrating product thinking, technical capability, and the 80/20 rule (delivering exactly what was needed without over-engineering) more effectively than any resume could. Lastly, the project achieved my learning goals - I gained hands-on experience with RAG architecture, Pinecone, semantic search, and modern Python development.
