Title: MattGPT: RAG Architecture & Semantic Search Implementation
Use Case(s): Design RAG architectures for conversational search across dense, unstructured content. Implement semantic retrieval systems when keyword matching fails to capture conceptual relevance.
Interview Questions: Tell me about implementing the RAG architecture for the Independent Project's semantic search system using Pinecone and OpenAI embeddings?, How did you integrate GPT-4o-mini for contextual response generation in the Independent Project's AI-driven semantic search solution?, Describe the process of using vector databases and LLM integration to make 130+ career stories semantically searchable during your 2025 sabbatical project?, Walk me through the error handling and confidence-based scoring mechanisms you implemented in the Independent Project's RAG architecture for semantic search?, How did you ensure the modular design of the RAG system allowed for easy experimentation and component swapping in the Independent Project?
Situation: With the UX design complete, I faced the technical challenge of making 130+ career stories truly searchable and conversationally accessible. Traditional keyword search would fail for several reasons: users might ask 'Have you led global teams?' but my stories might use terms like 'multinational workforce' or 'distributed teams across time zones - exact keyword matching would miss these semantic connections. Additionally, each story contained dense, multi-paragraph STAR narratives spanning hundreds of words, making simple text search impractical. I needed an architecture that could understand the semantic meaning of user questions, retrieve the most relevant stories based on conceptual similarity rather than keyword matching, and provide context-aware answers that cited specific sources. This required implementing a production-grade RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system with vector embeddings, semantic search, and LLM integration.
Task: Design and implement a complete RAG architecture for MattGPT that would: 1) Convert all 130+ career stories into vector embeddings that captured semantic meaning, not just keywords, 2) Enable fast, accurate semantic search using a production vector database (Pinecone), 3) Integrate with GPT-4o-mini to generate contextual, conversational responses using retrieved story content, 4) Implement intelligent source citation so users could see which specific stories informed each answer, 5) Build a data pipeline that could ingest new stories and update embeddings efficiently, and 6) Demonstrate production-grade AI engineering capabilities while delivering a practical, maintainable solution that avoided over-engineering common in AI projects.
Action: I architected a three-layer RAG system: data layer (JSONL story corpus), retrieval layer (Pinecone vector database), and generation layer (GPT-4o-mini integration).

For the data layer, I structured all 130+ stories in JSONL format with rich metadata fields: id, title, client, role, domain, industry_tags, competency_tags, technology_tags, and complete STAR narratives. Critically, I included the 5P Summary field as a distilled, searchable representation of each story's core value - this became essential for improving retrieval relevance since the summaries contained the most semantically dense information.

I implemented the embedding strategy using OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model (1536 dimensions). For each story, I constructed a single embedding text that prioritized the 5P Summary as the semantic core, augmented with industry context where available. This approach ensured embeddings captured the essence of each story while maintaining computational efficiency. When users asked about 'financial services experience,' the embeddings captured both explicit mentions in STAR narratives and implicit signals from industry and domain metadata.

For the retrieval layer, I configured Pinecone as the vector database, creating a serverless index with cosine similarity matching. I implemented semantic search with confidence-based scoring: queries are classified by a semantic router (0.80 hard accept, 0.72 soft accept thresholds), and results are gated by confidence levels (high ≥ 0.25, low ≥ 0.15). Metadata filtering allows users to filter by domain, industry, or technology.

For the generation layer, I integrated GPT-4o-mini with a carefully designed system prompt that instructed the model to: answer questions conversationally using retrieved story context, always cite specific stories using structured references, avoid hallucinating information not present in the retrieved context, and suggest relevant follow-up questions to guide further exploration. I built a citation tracking system that mapped responses back to specific story IDs with match percentage scores from the Pinecone similarity search.

I built error handling and fallback logic: if Pinecone was unavailable, the system gracefully degraded to local vector similarity using numpy; if GPT-4o-mini failed, it provided retrieved story titles and summaries without generated narrative. I documented the architecture with diagrams showing data flow from user query through embedding, retrieval, ranking, and generation.
Result: The RAG architecture enables semantic search that retrieves conceptually related stories beyond keyword matching. When users search for 'cloud migration experience,' the system retrieves stories about 'cloud-native architecture' and 'platform modernization' even when those exact terms aren't used, as demonstrated in the Ask MattGPT interface.

The semantic search implementation with confidence-based scoring allows users to ask natural language questions like 'What's your experience in financial services?' and receive relevant results filtered to banking and capital markets stories. The system retrieves JP Morgan Chase, HSBC, and other financial services projects with match scores displayed transparently.

The citation tracking system displays which stories informed each GPT-4o-mini response with Pinecone match percentage scores, providing transparency into the retrieval process. This also serves as a debugging tool - the system exposes retrieval details in debug mode, allowing me to see which stories were retrieved and adjust embedding or ranking strategies when needed.

I documented the complete RAG pipeline with architecture diagrams showing data flow from user query through embedding, retrieval, ranking, and generation. The modular design with clear separation between data layer (JSONL), retrieval layer (Pinecone), and generation layer (GPT-4o-mini) makes the architecture maintainable and allows for component experimentation.

The three-layer system uses Pinecone vector database for semantic search, OpenAI text-embedding-3-small embeddings for the story corpus, and GPT-4o-mini for contextual response generation with source citations. The architecture demonstrates practical AI engineering through confidence-based scoring, error handling that gracefully degrades when services are unavailable, and modular design that makes components easily swappable for experimentation.

Title: MattGPT: Strangler Fig Refactoring - From Monolith to Components
Use Case(s): Refactor monolithic applications into modular architectures without disrupting production systems. Apply strangler fig pattern to reduce technical debt incrementally while maintaining zero-downtime deployments.
Interview Questions: Tell me about the process of refactoring MattGPT's monolithic Streamlit application using the strangler fig pattern for Independent Project?, Describe the challenges you faced in managing technical debt and code organization in the MattGPT project and how you addressed them?, Walk me through the steps you took to eliminate 100% of code duplication and dead code in the MattGPT application?, How did you document the architectural decisions and quantitative metrics in the ARCHITECTURE.md file during the refactoring of MattGPT?
Situation: After launching the MattGPT MVP with functional RAG architecture and polished UX, I faced a classic technical debt problem: the main Streamlit application had grown to 5,765 lines in a single file (app.py), with an additional 2,100 lines in a legacy components file. What started as rapid prototyping to validate the product concept had evolved into a complex application with multiple views (Explore Stories, Ask MattGPT, About Matt), shared UI components (story cards, filters, chat interfaces), and intricate state management. The monolithic structure was becoming unmaintainable - making changes to one view risked breaking others, CSS selectors were bleeding across pages causing unintended styling conflicts, and the codebase violated basic software engineering principles like separation of concerns and single responsibility. Additionally, the size made code review difficult, and the lack of modularity meant I couldn't easily demonstrate specific technical capabilities in interviews without sharing massive files. I needed to refactor without disrupting the working application deployed on Streamlit Cloud or losing weeks to a complete rewrite.
Task: Refactor the MattGPT monolithic application into a maintainable, component-based architecture using the strangler fig pattern, which would allow incremental migration without breaking the production site. Specific goals included: 1) Extract and modularize pages (Explore Stories, Ask MattGPT, About Matt) into independent modules with clear boundaries, 2) Eliminate all duplicate functions scattered across files, 3) Remove dead code and commented legacy implementations, 4) Create shared utility modules for formatting, validation, scoring, and filtering logic, 5) Extract business logic into dedicated service layers for Pinecone and RAG operations, 6) Implement proper CSS scoping to prevent style conflicts across components, 7) Maintain full functionality throughout the refactoring process with zero downtime on the deployed Streamlit Cloud site, 8) Improve code organization to enable easier maintenance and faster feature development, and 9) Create clean, demonstrable code artifacts that could be reviewed in technical interviews. The refactoring needed to exemplify the 80/20 rule I advocate - deliver maximum improvement in code quality and maintainability with focused, systematic work.
Action: I adopted the strangler fig pattern — gradually replacing components while keeping the old system running. Five focused days in October 2025, three phases.

Phase 1 (Days 1-2): Infrastructure. Set up a clean directory structure separating UI components, pages, services, utilities, and config. Extracted hardcoded values — 47 instances of color values alone — into centralized config. Created shared CSS patterns to eliminate duplication across pages.

Phase 2 (Days 3-4): Page Extraction. Pulled each major page into standalone modules (explore_stories, ask_mattgpt, about_matt). 3,686 lines extracted from the monolith. Each extraction was tested locally, committed, deployed to Streamlit Cloud, and verified in production before moving to the next.

Phase 3 (Day 5): Deep cleanup. Took app.py from 3,234 lines to 1,014 — a clean router. Killed 12 duplicate functions, 8 zombie functions that were never called, and 430 lines of commented-out legacy code. Extracted Pinecone and RAG logic into dedicated services. Found and fixed three bugs that only surfaced during the cleanup. 12 atomic commits documenting every step.

Maintained full documentation in ARCHITECTURE.md throughout — directory structure, ADRs, CSS scoping patterns, migration progress, and metrics. Feature branches for major extractions, incremental testing, production verification after every deploy.
Result: The strangler fig refactoring delivered substantial improvements in code quality and maintainability without any production disruptions. I reduced app.py from 5,765 lines to 1,014 lines (82% reduction) over five focused days in October 2025, transforming it from a monolithic application into a clean router that orchestrates modular components and pages. The overall codebase decreased from approximately 11,400 lines to 6,250 lines (45% reduction) by eliminating all duplicate functions, dead code, and commented legacy implementations.

The modular architecture enables independent page development - explore_stories.py, ask_mattgpt.py, and about_matt.py can now be modified without risk of breaking other pages. I eliminated 100% of code duplication by extracting shared functionality into utils/ (548 lines of formatting, validation, scoring, filtering, and UI helpers) and services/ (479 lines of Pinecone and RAG business logic). The CSS scoping strategy documented in ARCHITECTURE.md resolved all style bleeding issues that had plagued the monolith.

The systematic refactoring approach using Git created an excellent audit trail: 12 atomic commits on October 22 alone documented the massive cleanup session, with clear commit messages explaining each change (fix bugs, remove duplicates, delete dead code, consolidate imports). The codebase achieved zero circular dependencies through careful extraction ordering and clear module boundaries.

The professional architecture documented in ARCHITECTURE.md provides a complete narrative of the refactoring process, including quantitative metrics (82% reduction in app.py, 45% overall codebase reduction), architectural decision records explaining CSS scoping and theme management, daily progress summaries, and file size breakdowns. This documentation serves both as a maintenance guide and as an interview artifact demonstrating systematic technical decision-making.

The refactoring validates my stated approach of incremental, focused improvements over risky rewrites - I delivered dramatic maintainability improvements through five days of systematic work while the application remained stable and functional throughout, deployed continuously to Streamlit Cloud with zero downtime.
public_tags: Architectural Documentation, CSS Scoping, CSS scoping, Code Duplication Elimination, Code Organization, Code Quality Improvement, Component Design, Component-Based Architecture, Engineering Best Practices, Git Branching, Git Version Control, Git version control, Incremental Refactoring, Modular Architecture, Modular Codebase Development, Python, Refactoring, Software Architecture, Software Engineering Principles, Strangler Fig Pattern, Streamlit, Streamlit Cloud, Systematic Problem Solving, Technical Debt Management, Technical Interview Preparation, Zero Downtime Deployment, Zero-Downtime Migration, architectural documentation, code duplication elimination, code maintainability, component-based architecture, component-based refactoring, incremental migration, incremental modernization, legacy system modernization, legacy system transformation, modular architecture, modular codebase, software architecture, software engineering principles, strangler fig pattern, systematic refactoring, technical debt management, zero downtime deployment, zero-downtime deployment
Purpose: To refactor a 5,765-line monolithic Streamlit application into maintainable component-based architecture using strangler fig pattern while maintaining zero downtime and demonstrating production-ready engineering practices
Performance: - Monolithic app.py reduced from 5,765 to 1,014 lines (82% reduction) through systematic five-day refactoring
- Overall codebase reduced from 11,400 to 6,250 lines (45% reduction)
- Zero code duplication achieved — 548 lines extracted to utils/, 479 lines to services/
- 100% dead code removed (700+ lines zombie functions, 430 lines commented legacy)
- Three critical bugs caught and fixed during cleanup
- Modular architecture (explore_stories.py, ask_mattgpt.py, about_matt.py) enabled independent page development
- Zero downtime maintained across continuous Streamlit Cloud deployments
- 12 atomic Git commits with clear audit trail documented the complete migration
Process: - Adopted strangler fig pattern for incremental five-day migration October 18-22, 2025

- Phase 1 (Days 1-2): created directory structure (ui/components/, ui/pages/, utils/, services/, config/), extracted theme constants eliminating 47 hardcoded values, created global_styles.py for shared CSS
- Phase 2 (Days 3-4): extracted pages to standalone modules (3,686 lines total), implemented defensive CSS scoping using first-child selectors, tested and deployed each extraction incrementally

- Phase 3 (Day 5): massive cleanup session reducing app.py from 3,234 to 1,014 lines by eliminating 12 duplicate functions, deleting 8 zombie functions, removing 430 lines commented code, extracting Pinecone and RAG to services/, creating five utils modules, fixing three critical bugs
- Used Git feature branches for major extractions
- Made 12 atomic commits documenting cleanup
- Documented architecture decisions, CSS patterns, metrics, and progress in ARCHITECTURE.md with ADR format
5PSummary: During five focused days in October 2025, I refactored MattGPT's monolithic 5,765-line Streamlit application into a maintainable component-based architecture using the strangler fig pattern. I reduced app.py by 82% to 1,014 lines, eliminated 100% of code duplication by extracting utilities and services, removed all dead code and legacy comments, and created independent page modules that can be modified without cross-contamination. The systematic approach maintained zero downtime across continuous deployments to Streamlit Cloud, produced 12 atomic Git commits documenting the cleanup process, and resulted in full ARCHITECTURE.md documentation with architectural decision records and quantitative metrics. The refactoring demonstrates practical 80/20 thinking - delivering dramatic maintainability improvements through focused, incremental work rather than risky rewrites.

Title: Solving RBC's 180-Day Data Visibility Problem
Use Case(s): Extend data visibility beyond platform limitations where clients need historical access for audits and forecasting. Build scalable reporting solutions when standard retention windows create operational and compliance gaps.
Interview Questions: Tell me about solving RBC's 180-day data visibility problem and how you designed the reporting solution?, How did you define requirements for extending reporting access beyond RBC's platform limitations?, Describe how you designed scheduled report generation at RBC to support audits and forecasting needs?, Walk me through your approach to client enablement and documentation for RBC's enhanced reporting capabilities?, How did you collaborate with product teams at RBC to design scalable data export functionality?
Situation: RBC's corporate banking platform limited transaction and chargeback history access to 180 days — creating real problems for corporate clients. Audit and compliance teams requiring up to 7 years of data were forced into manual requests for archived cold storage that took days or weeks to retrieve. Chargeback disputes cutting off right at the platform's history limit meant clients lacked the evidence trail for late-stage defenses, leading to lost revenue. Treasury teams couldn't perform year-over-year trend analysis or seasonal forecasting without downloading and stitching together monthly CSV files.
Task: I was tasked with assessing the data visibility limitation, defining requirements for extended reporting access, and designing scheduled report generation and export capabilities to empower clients with better financial oversight and audit-readiness.
Action: - Defined requirements for reporting tools, collaborating with product teams to design scheduled report generation and external data export functionalities
- Developed client enablement materials, including best practices and documentation to educate users on maximizing reporting capabilities
- Designed data accessibility approach, ensuring clients could retrieve extended historical records for audits, forecasting, and financial planning
Result: - Delivered requirements and solution design for extending data visibility beyond the 180-day platform limitation
- Scheduled report generation and scalable export capabilities addressed audit-readiness and forecasting needs that the 180-day limitation had blocked
- Client enablement materials provided the framework for adopting enhanced reporting capabilities

Title: Driving HSBC's Mortgage Transformation from Assessment to $10M Contract

- Drafted and negotiated the Statement of Work, managing back-and-forth with HSBC stakeholders to secure approval
- Presented findings and architectural recommendations to senior stakeholders, aligning business and IT teams on the transformation approach
Result: - Delivered full technology assessment with heat maps, gap analysis, and target state architecture leveraging Mortgage Cadence and Black Knight
- Automated workflows eliminated compliance-risk manual processes, closing regulatory gaps that had created exposure during audits
- Drove the engagement from ROM through detailed estimate, pricing model, staffing plan, and SOW — securing a $10M implementation contract
- Aligned HSBC business and IT leadership on a unified transformation approach from legacy to modern, compliant operations

Title: Modernizing Railroad Revenue:  Transitioning from Legacy to Cloud

Result: - Enabled real-time revenue tracking and adjustments based on dynamic shipment data, replacing batch-processed legacy reporting

Title: Modernizing What Matters Most through Value-Driven OKRs
Use Case(s): Accelerate legacy replatforming programs stalled by technical-debt-first prioritization. Apply OKR frameworks to align modernization sprints with business value instead of age or complexity.
Interview Questions: Describe the process of aligning cross-functional teams using value-driven sprints to achieve a reduction in delivery time for legacy replatforming?, Walk me through the methods you used to enhance stakeholder confidence and demonstrate long-term value during large-scale application modernization initiatives?
Situation: Many of Accenture's Cloud Innovation Center clients were embarking on large-scale application modernization initiatives to replatform legacy systems. The goals included enhancing scalability, improving operational efficiency, and reducing maintenance costs. Traditional processes across clients were often slow, lacked measurable goals, and faced challenges aligning stakeholder expectations with modernization objectives.
Task: Develop a rapid, measurable approach to replatform legacy applications, using OKR-driven sprints to ensure progress transparency and success alignment. This approach needed to streamline delivery cycles while maintaining high standards of quality and compliance.
Action: - Established week-long sprints focused on replatforming applications, aligning each sprint with clear OKRs such as the number of applications transitioned or user stories completed
- Introduced CI/CD pipelines to automate testing and deployment, minimizing manual QA efforts and accelerating transitions to production environments
- Developed a library of reusable templates and patterns, allowing teams to standardize replatforming processes across multiple applications, ensuring consistency and reducing implementation time
- Conducted regular sprint planning and review sessions to align technical teams, stakeholders, and business goals, ensuring transparency and collaboration throughout the modernization process
Result: - Applications were replatformed within days rather than weeks, significantly accelerating the modernization timeline
- Automated pipelines enabled continuous releases, improving operational efficiency and scalability
- OKR-driven transparency fostered trust, as stakeholders could track progress and see direct alignment with organizational goals
- The reusable patterns and automated processes set a foundation for future modernization efforts, reducing costs and improving team productivity

Title: What I Learned About Assumptions
Solution / Offering: Leadership Philosophy, Risk Management, IAM Best Practices
Use Case(s): Answer interview questions about failure, mistakes, and lessons learned. Demonstrate accountability and growth when discussing risk ownership and verification gaps.
Interview Questions: Tell me about a time you failed?, Describe a mistake you made that impacted customers and what you learned from it?, Tell me about a time your assumptions led to a problem — how did you handle it?, What's the biggest lesson you've learned from a delivery failure?, How has a past mistake changed the way you approach risk and environment management?
Situation: I was the tech lead on a global platform modernization delivery for a Fortune 500 financial services client with multiple environments: dev, QA, UAT, and production. The program was under delivery pressure with offshore teams executing test cycles across environments. IAM controls, environment segregation, and downstream access configurations had been inherited from prior phases with existing documentation.
Task: My responsibility was to ensure a safe environment setup, but I relied on assumptions rather than verifying the IAM configuration and downstream access controls.
Action: - Assumed IAM and environment boundaries were already correct
- Focused on delivery pressures instead of validating access patterns
- Relied on inherited environment documentation without confirming accuracy
- Did not audit who could trigger downstream jobs or email processes
- Did not implement compensating controls (data masking, email interception)
- Did not verify whether production customer data (names, email addresses) had been masked or scrubbed in downstream environments
- Took accountability for the incident and led the team through root cause analysis and remediation
- Worked collectively with the team to understand what happened and implement corrective controls
Result: An offshore tester accidentally triggered an email job in UAT containing unmasked production customer data — sending communications to thousands of real clients. The root cause was insufficient IAM checks, unclear environment boundaries, and production data that had not been scrubbed from downstream environments — assumptions I should have verified rather than inherited. I took accountability and led the team through root cause analysis and remediation. Unfortunately, the client demanded the tester be removed from the project — a systemic failure that resulted in an individual consequence, which reinforced my belief that leaders own the environment their teams operate in.
public_tags: IAM, IAM controls, Stakeholder management, a mistake I made, access control, access control verification, accountability, change management, communication skills, compensating controls, data protection, delivery pressure management, environment segregation, escalation, escalation process, failure, financial services, identity and access management, incident prevention, incident resolution, leadership development, lessons learned, mistake, my biggest failure, offshore team coordination, ownership, platform delivery, process improvement, risk management, root cause analysis, team collaboration, technical leadership, tell me about a time I failed, tell me about a time you failed, trust-building, verification and validation, what went wrong
Purpose: Demonstrate accountability and growth — showing that assumptions are risks in disguise, and verification is a leadership responsibility.
Performance: - Established personal standard: if I see a risk that could blow up, I own it until it's resolved or formally accepted at the right level
- IAM and environment hygiene are now day-one priorities in my delivery approach
- Assumptions are risks in disguise — verification is a leadership responsibility, not a checkbox
- Data masking and PII scrubbing in non-production environments are now non-negotiable prerequisites before any test cycle begins
Process: - Identify risks early and document them clearly
- If deprioritized, escalate or propose compensating controls
- Don't let delivery pressure override risk judgment
- Treat IAM, environment segregation, and data protection as non-negotiable foundations
5PSummary: When asked about a time I failed, this is my story. I assumed our IAM controls, environment boundaries, and data masking were correct on a Fortune 500 platform delivery — and an offshore tester accidentally triggered an email job in UAT that sent communications containing unmasked production data to thousands of real clients. I took accountability, led the team through root cause analysis and remediation, and learned that assumptions are risks in disguise. IAM validation, environment verification, PII scrubbing, and risk ownership are now day-one leadership responsibilities in my delivery approach.

Title: Partnering with Clients to Build the Right Product Faster
Use Case(s): Partner with clients to validate product concepts where waterfall approaches cause months of wasted effort. Embed clients into balanced teams using hypothesis-driven development and rapid user feedback to adapt to shifting requirements without sacrificing quality.
Interview Questions:
Tell me about how you partnered with Fortune 500 clients to break the traditional waterfall cycle?
How did you utilize hypothesis-driven design and Build-Measure-Learn loops to reduce time-to-market?
How did you implement iterative release frameworks to reduce change requests and bugs?
Describe how you engaged stakeholders and used rapid user feedback to enhance user satisfaction and enable confident pivots?
Walk me through achieving faster delivery while maintaining high-quality standards??
Situation: Traditional waterfall consulting kept clients at arm's length — they handed over requirements, teams built in isolation, and misalignment surfaced late. When product requirements shifted due to changing priorities or market conditions, these teams had no mechanism to adapt without costly rework or sacrificing quality and predictability.
Task: Introduce a product-discipline framework where none existed, enabling clients to validate concepts quickly through MVPs, iterative testing, and feedback loops — replacing ad hoc delivery with a repeatable model that reduced rework and maintained alignment with evolving business goals.
Action: - Adopted Lean Product Principles emphasizing the Build-Measure-Learn loop for rapid iterations and data-driven decisions

- Applied Hypothesis-Driven Development (HDD), partnering with clients to frame and test key hypotheses using user research and market data, ensuring MVPs included only essential features to validate critical assumptions
- Broke development into small batch workflows enabling continuous validation and faster feedback cycles

Title: Testing Assumptions Before Committing Resources with Hypothesis-Driven Design

Title: Rescuing and Stabilizing JP Morgan's TS Dynamics CRM Program
Use Case(s): Rescue a failing Microsoft Dynamics CRM implementation for JP Morgan's Treasury & Securities Services — stabilize vendor delivery, fix code quality, transform the engagement model from offshore factory to onsite staff augmentation, and execute knowledge transfer to JPM's internal team.
Interview Questions: Tell me about a time you were brought in to rescue a failing program — what was the situation with JP Morgan's TS Dynamics CRM and how did you stabilize it?, How did you diagnose and fix the root cause of Avanade's delivery failures on the JP Morgan Dynamics CRM program?, Describe how you transitioned Avanade from an offshore factory model to onsite staff augmentation — what drove that decision and what changed?, Walk me through managing CIO-level stakeholder expectations during a program crisis with four organizations involved — Accenture Avanade Microsoft and JP Morgan?, How did you execute the knowledge transfer from Avanade to JP Morgan's internal team while maintaining delivery continuity?
Situation: JP Morgan's Treasury & Securities Services (now Corporate Investment Bank) was implementing Microsoft Dynamics CRM for sales opportunity management — pipeline tracking, client relationships, and revenue visibility for the TSS sales organization. Avanade was the development vendor, but the program was in crisis. Avanade had missed all major milestones, accumulated a large volume of high-severity defects, and demonstrated a fundamental lack of understanding of JPM's requirements — with no clear evidence they had validated their interpretation before building. The situation had escalated to 3x daily stabilization calls between Accenture, Avanade, Microsoft, and JP Morgan project leadership, plus 2-3x weekly executive meetings involving JPM TSS CIO Guy Shapiro, Microsoft senior leadership, Avanade top leadership, and Accenture's JP Morgan client account leader. I was pulled in to help turn it around.
Task: Stabilize the failing program, fix the vendor delivery model, remediate code quality, and ultimately transition the platform to JP Morgan's internal team. Dual role as Program Manager (release planning, scoping, QA, deployments) and Development Manager (directing Avanade development resources, fixing quality, managing the vendor model transformation).
Action: - Took over program management and development management for a program in active crisis — 3x daily stabilization calls, CIO-level escalation, vendor credibility near zero

- Avanade's standalone offshore factory model had produced work disconnected from JPM's actual requirements — no effective requirements validation, no shared understanding of acceptance criteria, resulting in missed milestones and high-severity defects

- Orchestrated the transition from Avanade's standalone offsite factory delivery model to an onsite staff augmentation model — embedding Avanade resources side-by-side with JPM teams to fix the communication breakdown and align on requirements in real time
- Directed work efforts of 20-25 Avanade resources, establishing procedures to remediate defects and stabilize code quality to meet delivery targets
- Spearheaded Agile methodology adoption — stand-ups, retrospectives, iteration planning — replacing the waterfall approach that had contributed to the milestone failures and communication gaps
- Managed release planning, road mapping, scoping, QA, performance testing, infrastructure, and deployments to regain delivery predictability
- Managed the CIO-level stakeholder cadence — ensuring Guy Shapiro and executive leadership from Microsoft, Avanade, and Accenture had accurate status visibility as the program stabilized

- Executed the planned knowledge transition from Avanade to JPM's internal team — KT documentation, sessions, source code handoff, and managed resource ramp-down — enabling JPM to own and operate the platform long-term
- Paired Avanade developers with JPM developers during the transition, building internal capability while maintaining delivery continuity
Result: Stabilized a program that was in active crisis with CIO-level escalation — moved from 3x daily stabilization calls to predictable delivery cadence. The vendor model transformation from offshore factory to onsite staff augmentation fixed the requirements disconnect that had caused the original failures. Agile adoption replaced waterfall processes that had masked delivery risk. Defect volume and severity dropped as the remediation procedures took hold. Successfully executed the full knowledge transfer to JPM's internal team — KT documentation, source code, paired development — enabling JPM to take the platform in-house for long-term ownership. The engagement model and transition approach became a reference point for how to recover a failing vendor delivery.
public_tags: Agile Adoption, Agile Transformation, CIO-Level Stakeholder Management, CRM Implementation, Code Quality Remediation, Defect Remediation, Development Management, Dynamics CRM, Knowledge Transfer, Microsoft Dynamics, Milestone Recovery, Offshore-to-Onsite Transformation, Program Recovery, Program Turnaround, Release Planning, Staff Augmentation, Stakeholder management, Vendor Delivery Failure, Vendor Model Transformation, Vendor Recovery, agile adoption, change management, crisis management, cross-functional collaboration, defect remediation, financial services transformation, knowledge transfer, onsite staff augmentation, program stabilization, release planning, requirements validation, team dynamics, trust-building, vendor management
Purpose: Rescue a failing Dynamics CRM implementation — stabilize vendor delivery, fix quality, transform engagement model, and transfer ownership to JPM
Performance: - Stabilized from 3x daily crisis calls to predictable delivery
- Vendor model transformed from offshore factory to onsite staff aug — restoring delivery accountability
- Defect backlog remediated
- Full KT executed to JPM internal team, enabling self-sustaining operation
Process: - Diagnosed requirements disconnect, transitioned Avanade onsite
- Directed 20-25 developers, introduced agile ceremonies
- Managed CIO-level escalation cadence, executed knowledge transfer and resource ramp-down
5PSummary: JP Morgan's Dynamics CRM implementation for Treasury & Securities Services was in crisis — Avanade had missed all milestones with high-severity defects, triggering 3x daily stabilization calls and CIO-level escalation. I was pulled in to turn it around: transitioned Avanade from offshore factory to onsite staff augmentation, directed 20-25 developers through defect remediation, drove agile adoption, and executed full knowledge transfer to JPM's internal team. Stabilized the program to predictable delivery and enabled JPM to own the platform long-term.

Title: Delivering Global Data Conversion for JP Morgan's TS Dynamics CRM Across Four Regions
Use Case(s): Migrate sales opportunity management data from a proprietary legacy system to Microsoft Dynamics CRM across North America, Latin America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific — with data cleansing, scripted migration, validation testing, and compliance verification across jurisdictions with different data privacy, financial reporting, and customer interaction regulations.
Interview Questions: Tell me about migrating JP Morgan's legacy sales data to Dynamics CRM across four regions — what made the data conversion complex?, How did you establish the command center approach for regional go-lives and what did it look like in practice?, Describe the data cleansing challenges migrating from a proprietary legacy system to Dynamics CRM — what kinds of quality issues did you encounter?, Walk me through how you managed compliance requirements across North America Latin America EMEA and Asia Pacific during the CRM data migration?, How did you ensure JPM's sales teams maintained pipeline visibility during the cutover from the legacy system to Dynamics CRM?
Situation: JP Morgan's Treasury & Securities Services sales organization operated on a proprietary, custom-built legacy system for pipeline and opportunity management. The migration to Microsoft Dynamics CRM required converting this data across four regions — North America, Latin America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific — each with distinct data privacy regulations, financial reporting standards, and customer interaction rules. Asia and Switzerland were the most stringent — Singapore's MAS banking secrecy and cross-border data transfer restrictions, South Korea's FSC/FSS data localization mandates, Japan's FSA data handling requirements, and Switzerland's FINMA geographic access controls and data protection requirements — with compliance complexities in these markets painful enough to delay or postpone regional rollouts. A CRM holding client relationship data, pipeline intelligence, and revenue details was squarely in scope for each regulator. The legacy data itself was messy: inconsistent formats, quality issues, and no standardized schema across regions. A failed migration would mean sales teams losing visibility into their pipeline during the cutover — unacceptable for a revenue-generating function.
Task: Establish and execute the data conversion strategy — from legacy extraction through cleansing, transformation, validation, and regional go-live — ensuring data integrity across all four regions while meeting jurisdiction-specific compliance requirements for data privacy, financial reporting, and customer data handling.
Action: - Established the data conversion strategy and implementation approach for migrating from the proprietary legacy system to Dynamics CRM across North America, Latin America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific
- Designed and executed scripted migration processes with extensive data cleansing to address quality issues in the legacy data — inconsistent formats, missing fields, duplicate records
- scripted extraction, transformation, cleansing, test runs, validation, and final cutover — executed iteratively across regions
- Established a command center approach for regional go-lives — coordinating cutover activities, real-time issue resolution, and stakeholder communication during each regional deployment
- Partnered with local compliance teams to identify region-specific requirements for data privacy, financial reporting standards, and customer data regulations in each jurisdiction
- Conducted compliance validation as part of UAT to confirm the migrated data and platform configuration met regional audit requirements before each regional launch
- Established continuous compliance monitoring framework to track regulatory changes and adjust rollout plans across the four regions
- Managed the sequencing of regional go-lives based on data readiness and compliance sign-off — threading dependencies into the overall delivery timeline
Result: Delivered successful data conversion from the proprietary legacy system to Dynamics CRM across all four regions — North America, Latin America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific — without loss of pipeline visibility for JPM's TSS sales organization. The command center approach enabled coordinated regional go-lives with real-time issue resolution during cutover. Data cleansing and validation processes caught quality issues before they reached production. Compliance validation confirmed the platform met regional data privacy and financial reporting requirements in each jurisdiction. The CRM gave JPM's TSS sales teams unified pipeline tracking, client relationship management, and revenue visibility across all four regions for the first time on a single platform.

Purpose: Migrate legacy sales opportunity data to Dynamics CRM across four regions with data integrity and compliance verification per jurisdiction.
Performance: - Compliant deployments achieved across APAC, EMEA, and the Americas — Asia and Switzerland meeting the most stringent data residency requirements
- Compliance-driven delays in Asia and Switzerland absorbed without derailing overall program timeline
- Customer and pipeline data safeguarded across jurisdictions through proactive compliance management
- Unified pipeline tracking achieved on single platform for the first time across all four regions
Process: - Established scripted migration with cleansing/validation cycles across four regions
- Partnered with local compliance teams to identify region-specific requirements including data privacy laws, reporting standards, and financial regulations
- Provided strategic input on CRM configuration needs, ensuring the system could adapt to different compliance landscapes without disrupting core operations
- Conducted compliance validation as part of UAT to confirm the platform met regional audit requirements before each launch
- Established continuous compliance monitoring framework, tracking regulatory changes and adjusting rollout plans across APAC, EMEA, Latin America, and North America
- Established command center approach for regional go-lives with real-time issue resolution
- Sequenced rollouts based on data readiness and compliance sign-off — absorbing delays in Asia and Switzerland without derailing the overall timeline
5PSummary: JP Morgan's TSS sales organization needed to migrate from a proprietary legacy system to Dynamics CRM across North America, Latin America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific — with messy legacy data and stringent compliance requirements per region, particularly in Asia and Switzerland where data residency and visibility rules delayed rollouts. I established the data conversion strategy with scripted migration, data cleansing, and a command center approach for regional go-lives — while providing strategic input on CRM configuration per jurisdiction to meet regulatory requirements. Delivered successful conversion across all four regions with compliance validation per jurisdiction, giving TSS sales teams unified pipeline visibility for the first time.

Title: Estimating by Risk and Complexity Instead of Time
Use Case(s): Apply risk- and complexity-based estimation in high-uncertainty delivery environments. Restore stakeholder confidence when time-based estimation causes repeated overruns.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you introduced risk- and complexity-based estimation workshops at Accenture?, How did you develop and implement a complexity matrix to enhance estimation accuracy?, Describe shifting estimation from time-based to complexity-based and its impact on stakeholder confidence?, Walk me through how early identification of high-risk tasks reduced delays and rework?, How did complexity-based estimations enhance stakeholder communication and transparency?
Situation: Accenture teams often relied on time-based estimation, which struggled to capture the complexities and risks inherent in modern application development. This resulted in frequent overruns, misaligned resource allocation, and reduced stakeholder confidence due to unpredictable delivery timelines
Task: Shift the estimation approach to focus on assessing risk and complexity instead of time, enabling the team to identify potential obstacles early and allocate resources more effectively to manage these challenges
Action: - Introduced workshops where teams discussed user stories and assigned complexity points based on factors like technical difficulty, domain knowledge required, and potential blockers
- Created a matrix that categorized tasks by low, medium, or high complexity and risk, providing a shared language for estimation that emphasized underlying challenges over time
- Placed high-risk, high-complexity tasks in earlier sprints to identify issues early and allocate adequate resources, reducing the risk of late-stage surprises
- Built periodic reassessment into sprints to capture any changes in risk or complexity as tasks evolved, adjusting sprint goals accordingly
Result: - By focusing on risk and complexity, the team achieved higher alignment between estimates and actual task requirements, leading to fewer overruns
- Early identification of high-risk tasks allowed the team to proactively address challenges, decreasing project delays and reducing rework
- Estimations based on complexity helped stakeholders understand the nature of tasks, enhancing transparency and setting realistic expectations
- By understanding task challenges upfront, the team could tackle complex work with better preparation, improving satisfaction

Title: Scaling Development Capacity with Modern Engineering Practices
Use Case(s): Scale development capacity for teams overwhelmed by regulatory pressure and heavy workloads. Embed Lean XP and automation practices to deliver quality at pace when internal teams face burnout and deadline pressure.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you used Lean XP practices to scale development capacity at Capital One while ensuring defect-free code delivery?, How did you integrate automated testing and continuous integration to achieve 4x the output of Capital One's internal teams?, Describe the process of building client trust with Capital One through modern engineering practices and the outcomes it led to?, Walk me through the challenges you faced in embedding cross-functional team leadership at Capital One and how you overcame them?, How did you demonstrate the resilience and strategic impact of modern engineering to secure additional engagements with Capital One?
Situation: Capital One faced significant pressure to meet regulatory demands while managing heavy development workloads. Internal teams were overwhelmed, experiencing burnout, and struggling to maintain quality under tight deadlines. Scaling efficiently without compromising quality was critical.
Task: As Director of Accenture's Cloud Innovation Center, I was responsible for leading the initiative to rapidly augment Capital One's development capacity. My goal was to implement modern engineering practices that enabled high-quality, defect-free software at scale, while demonstrating the long-term value of Lean XP methodologies.
Action: - Designed and led the implementation of modern engineering practices, integrating Lean XP principles to accelerate high-quality development
- Built a balanced, cross-functional team, combining engineers, product managers, and user-centered designers to maximize collaboration and agile execution
- Applied advanced practices, such as pair programming and continuous integration, ensuring rapid, defect-free delivery aligned with regulatory and business needs
- Developed an automated testing suite, establishing scalable, high-velocity quality assurance throughout the development cycle
Result: - Delivered defect-free production code at 4x the output compared to Capital One's internal teams, significantly improving development efficiency
- Proved the resilience and strategic impact of modern engineering, earning client trust and recognition for Accenture's Cloud Innovation Center
- Secured additional Capital One engagements, reinforcing Accenture's leadership in digital transformation and development acceleration

Title: Scaling Talent and Practices for Agile Product Innovation
Use Case(s): Scale talent pipelines for cloud-native delivery by hiring, onboarding, and skilling professionals in modern engineering practices. Embed user-centered design and lean product development as foundational principles for new teams.
Interview Questions: Tell me about scaling talent from scratch for Accenture's Cloud Innovation Center (CIC)s in Atlanta and Tampa?, How did you recruit for mindset and potential when hiring 150+ professionals?, Describe how you designed onboarding and enablement programs that blended technical training with user-centered methodologies?, How did you achieve minimal attrition while scaling teams rapidly across two locations?, How did you embed UCD and XP practices as standard ways of working for new hires with varying experience levels?
Situation: To meet growing client demand for cloud-native solutions, Accenture needed to expand its cloud transformation capabilities by establishing two new Cloud Innovation Centers in Atlanta and Tampa. These centers would deliver bespoke solutions to clients, leveraging modern engineering and CX-focused practices. The challenge was twofold: recruiting and onboarding a large number of new hires who lacked hands-on experience with cloud-native technologies or Agile ways of working, and training these teams to adopt user-centered methodologies that ensured all solutions prioritized customer needs and rapid delivery.
Task: Build and scale CIC teams by recruiting talent with diverse backgrounds, onboarding and upskilling them in user-centered design and modern engineering practices, and creating a culture of continuous learning and experimentation
Action: - Talent Acquisition and Recruitment:
- Partnered with recruitment teams to source high-potential graduates and experienced professionals, focusing on diversity and collaborative mindsets
- Hired over 150 professionals, emphasizing enthusiasm for learning and adaptability to modern engineering practices
- Streamlined Onboarding and Enablement:
- Designed an onboarding program tailored to CIC needs, blending technical training with user-centered methodologies like design thinking and CX-driven requirements gathering
- Conducted workshops on Lean Product Development, Hypothesis-Driven Design (HDD), and Build-Measure-Learn cycles, enabling teams to deliver MVPs aligned with user needs
- Introduced XP practices such as pair programming, continuous delivery, and iterative feedback loops, ensuring teams worked collaboratively to maintain high quality
- Embedding User-Centered Design and CX Excellence:
- Led immersive training on UCD principles, emphasizing stakeholder interviews, user research, and persona development to ground all solutions in customer needs
- Organized CX workshops to align cross-functional teams around shared goals and prioritize features based on user impact
- Integrated rapid prototyping and usability testing into project workflows, ensuring end-user validation at every stage
- Competency Frameworks and Career Development:
- Developed a skills matrix to track and measure individual progress in cloud technologies, Agile practices, and CX methodologies
- Paired junior hires with experienced mentors to accelerate skill development and instill confidence in working with complex systems
- Fostered a culture of continuous improvement by embedding retrospectives and learnings into team practices
- Ways of Working Transformation:
- Established cross-functional teams that combined user-centered designers, product managers, and engineers, ensuring holistic collaboration
- Embedded TDD and CI/CD practices into workflows to improve reliability and reduce defects
- Promoted psychological safety, encouraging teams to experiment, fail fast, and iterate to achieve the best outcomes
Result: - Practitioners progressed from guided to independent delivery through structured "I do, we do, you do" learning model — not just trained on concepts but capable of executing at CIC standard without ongoing oversight
- Teams consistently developed MVPs within weeks, using Build-Measure-Learn cycles to validate features with end-users and align with business goals
- Achieved near-zero attrition in the first two years, with multiple hires promoted due to their readiness and high performance

- Designed onboarding program blending technical training with user-centered methodologies
- Introduced XP practices, lean product development, and Build-Measure-Learn cycles

- Developed skills matrix to track individual and team progress

I recruited for mindset and potential, designed tailored onboarding and enablement programs, embedded UCD, lean product, and XP practices as standard ways of working, and fostered psychological safety and continuous learning. This scaled the team to 150+ professionals with minimal attrition, with teams adopting modern engineering practices that carried through to client delivery.

Title: Shifting Client Focus from What They Build to How They Work
Use Case(s): Identify operating model mismatches in clients modernizing technology but preserving legacy team structures and IT-centric ways of working. Lead workshops that surface dysfunction patterns and shift organizations from project-driven IT delivery to product-centric operating models where teams own outcomes, not outputs.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you shifted client organizations from IT-centric to product-centric operating models?, Describe how you used coaching labs and 1:1 pairing to change how client teams worked not just what they built., How did you help leadership see the gap between wanting agile and how their organization was actually structured?, Walk me through how clients adopted self-regulating balanced teams and sustained those practices after the engagement ended., How did restructuring around business domains and new ways of working lead to 15-35% cost reductions?
Situation: Clients often hired us to modernize their technology but kept organizing teams and making decisions the same way they always had. They were recreating legacy dysfunction in new systems — same silos, same handoffs, same bottlenecks — and wondering why transformation felt so hard. The technology wasn't the problem. The operating model was.
Task: Help leadership teams shift from IT-centric to product-centric operating models — restructuring teams around business domains, enabling self-regulating balanced teams, and establishing new ways of working before legacy habits became permanent in new architecture
Action: - Delivered Product-Centric Operating Model Playbook workshops — assessed organizational maturity and charted a course forward across methodology, governance, team organization, and funding
- Ran Agility Coaching Labs — immersive 6-week programs working with client teams on their actual work to improve agile and DevOps practices
- Coached client teams 1:1 using "I do, we do, you do" model — pairing to bring teams to a product mindset and new ways of working
- Helped leadership see the gap between "we want agile" and how they were actually organized — showed how team structure was shaping their architecture
- Created shared language and alignment across business and technology stakeholders
- Established success criteria tied to ways of working, not just delivery milestones
- minimize time to value, solve for need, excel at change, understand the product is never done
Result: - Reduced costs 15-35% by restructuring from traditional IT delivery to lean, self-regulating balanced teams aligned to business domains
- Prevented costly rework by catching operating model mismatches before they became technical debt
- Clients adopted balanced teams, OKRs, and lean product practices that outlasted the engagement
- Improved stakeholder alignment between business and IT, reducing mid-project friction
- Clients left with clarity on how to sustain change, not just what to build

Title: Shifting Teams from Velocity to Value
Use Case(s): Align delivery teams around value-based prioritization when velocity metrics are misused as targets. Improve estimation practices in environments where unpredictable timelines erode stakeholder trust.
Interview Questions: Tell me about how you shifted teams from focusing on velocity to value at Accenture?, How did you implement value-based delivery practices to improve estimation accuracy?, Describe reinforcing stakeholder alignment and improving team engagement?, Walk me through establishing a continuous improvement culture using velocity tracking and retrospectives?, How did you prioritize value-based outcomes to enhance feature relevance?
Situation: Without a framework for prioritization and value-based metrics, teams faced unpredictable timelines and misaligned project outcomes. Traditional measures like velocity were sometimes misused as targets rather than insights for continuous improvement, leading to inefficiencies and missed opportunities to focus on high-impact work. This challenge limited the team's ability to balance user needs, business goals, and sustainable delivery practices.
Task: Introduce a structured approach to prioritize user stories based on value, refine estimation methods, and reframe metrics like velocity as tools for learning and adaptation, rather than rigid performance targets. These changes aimed to align delivery cycles with business objectives and enable continuous improvement.
Action: - Educated teams and stakeholders on using metrics like velocity as diagnostic tools to identify trends and enable continuous improvement, rather than as strict performance goals
- Developed and implemented a framework to prioritize user stories based on business impact and user value, ensuring alignment with strategic goals focusing on delivering value in each iteration
- Conducted workshops to improve estimation accuracy through techniques like planning poker, emphasizing factors such as risk, complexity, and value to refine story points and improve planning
- Established regular retrospectives to revisit and adapt prioritization and metrics based on team progress, ensuring flexibility and responsiveness to evolving needs
- Engaged stakeholders to align on value-based metrics, showcasing how these metrics supported decision-making and sustainable delivery
Result: - Better estimation accuracy resulted in fewer missed deadlines and better resource allocation
- Velocity tracking and retrospective insights fed forward into future estimations and priorities, compounding accuracy gains over successive sprints
- Prioritizing impactful outcomes improved feature relevance and alignment with business goals, increasing stakeholder and end-user satisfaction
- Shifted velocity metrics from being misinterpreted as targets to becoming tools for insight, fostering a culture of learning and adaptation
- Empowered teams through transparent prioritization and flexible planning, improving engagement and sustainable delivery practices

Purpose: Align delivery cycles with business objectives and enable continuous improvement
Performance: - Improved delivery predictability and efficiency
- Fostered a continuous improvement culture
- Increased feature relevance and business alignment
- Reduced misuse of metrics
- Boosted team morale through transparent prioritization
Process: - Reframed metrics for insight
- Value-based prioritization
- Enhanced estimation practices
- Integrated continuous feedback
- Stakeholder alignment on metrics
5PSummary: Without a framework for prioritization, teams faced unpredictable timelines and misaligned outcomes — velocity was misused as a target rather than an insight for improvement. I reframed velocity, prioritized value-based outcomes, and reinforced stakeholder alignment. Estimation accuracy improved, product relevance increased, and team engagement strengthened.

Title: Simplifying MattGPT's RAG Pipeline Through Entity Gate Removal
Use Case(s): Identify when accumulated complexity hurts system performance. Use eval-driven development to make data-informed decisions about removing features rather than adding more.
Interview Questions: Tell me about a time you simplified a system by removing features., How do you decide when to cut scope vs. add capability?, Describe a situation where your initial approach was wrong and how you course-corrected., How do you use testing or evaluation to make architectural decisions?
Situation: After several months of iterative development, MattGPT's retrieval pipeline had accumulated significant complexity. The system included entity detection to identify client names and project references in queries, confidence gating with multiple threshold levels, query rewriting to improve semantic matches, and a semantic router with intent classification. Each feature had been added to solve a specific problem, but the interactions between them created unpredictable behavior. Valid queries were getting blocked by overly aggressive confidence gates. Entity detection was triggering false positives, causing the system to search for clients that weren't mentioned. Debugging sessions that should have taken minutes stretched into hours because tracing a query through multiple routing paths required understanding five different subsystems. The codebase had grown to the point where I couldn't confidently predict how a new query would be handled.
Task: Determine whether the accumulated complexity was delivering value proportional to its cost. If features weren't earning their keep, remove them cleanly without breaking the core retrieval and response quality that users depended on. Establish a rigorous, data-driven approach to making this decision rather than relying on intuition or anecdote.
Action: - Built a golden query eval suite with 61 real questions representing what recruiters and hiring managers actually ask — questions like "What's your experience with payments systems?" and "Tell me about a time you led a struggling team"
- synthesis questions (themes, patterns), surgical questions (specific projects), and edge cases (ambiguous, off-topic)
- Ran the full eval suite against the current complex system to establish a baseline — documented pass/fail for each query with notes on failure modes
- Created a feature flag to disable the entity gate entirely without removing code
- Re-ran the complete eval suite with entity detection disabled
- 98% pass rate with entity gate vs. 98% pass rate without
- Analyzed the few queries where behavior differed — in every case, the simpler system performed as well or better
- Removed 132 lines of entity detection code, including the entity extraction logic, the gate conditions, and the associated routing paths
- Re-ran evals one final time to confirm no regression
- Documented the decision and rationale in the codebase
Result: - Eval pass rate held steady at 98% after removing entity detection
- Response quality unchanged — users couldn't distinguish between before and after
- Debugging time dropped significantly — with fewer routing paths, I could trace any query in minutes instead of hours
- Codebase became 132 lines lighter and substantially easier to reason about
- Established eval-driven development as a core practice — no feature changes without running the suite
- Validated that sometimes the best engineering decision is deletion, not addition
public_tags: Codebase Simplification, Complexity Reduction, Data-Driven Decision Making, Debugging Efficiency, Engineering Best Practices, Eval-Driven Development, Feature Flagging, Hypothesis-Driven Refactoring, LLM Prompt Engineering, Python, RAG Architecture, Semantic Search, Software Modernization, Stakeholder Management, Technical Debt Reduction
Person: Engineers and technical leaders evaluating AI/ML architecture decisions
Purpose: Demonstrate that simplification is a valid engineering outcome — sometimes the best feature is the one you delete, and data-driven eval suites enable confident decision-making about technical debt
Performance: - 98% eval pass rate maintained post-removal
- 132 lines of code deleted
- Debugging cycles reduced from hours to minutes
- Zero regression in response quality
Process: - Build full eval suite before making changes
- Establish baseline metrics with current system
- Use feature flags to test removal safely
- Compare results quantitatively, not anecdotally
- Only commit removal when data supports the decision
- Document rationale for future reference
5PSummary: MattGPT's RAG pipeline had accumulated complexity over months of development — entity detection, confidence gating, query rewriting, and multiple routing paths. Edge cases kept surfacing, and debugging took hours because I couldn't predict how queries would flow through the system. I built a 61-query eval suite covering real recruiter questions, established a baseline, then disabled the entity gate and re-ran the suite. Results were identical: 98% pass rate with or without the complexity. I deleted 132 lines of code, debugging became straightforward, and response quality stayed the same. The experience reinforced that eval-driven development enables confident simplification — sometimes the best feature is the one you remove.

Title: Delivering Multi-Client Customization and Compliance for the White-Label Card Portal
Use Case(s): Deliver white-label platforms serving multiple clients with divergent branding, functional, and compliance requirements. Lead ADA compliance efforts where internal teams lack accessibility expertise.
Interview Questions: Tell me about a time you had to become a subject matter expert in an unfamiliar domain under time pressure?, Describe leading an accessibility compliance effort across a multi-client platform with no prior ADA expertise on the team?, Walk me through refactoring a UI for screen reader compliance across onshore and offshore teams?, How did you manage daily coordination across time zones while simultaneously learning and implementing new standards?, Tell me about establishing development guidelines that outlasted the initial project?
Situation: Fiserv's white-label card portal — the shared platform behind branded credit card sites for retailers like Home Depot, Citi, and Meijer, where customers log in to manage and pay their store credit card accounts — needed to serve each client with unique branding, functional, and compliance requirements. The DOJ had begun aggressively enforcing ADA Title III web compliance with financial portals as primary targets, and Canada's AODA mandated public website compliance by 2014. The project had no accessibility expertise on the team, exposing the portal to significant legal and regulatory risk across every client brand.
Task: Delivered a customizable, ACA/ADA-compliant card portal to support enterprise clients' unique branding, accessibility, and integration requirements, ensuring reliable deployment across multiple financial institutions.
Action: - On top of program responsibilities, became the ADA/AODA Subject Matter Expert overnight — learned WCAG 2.0/2.1 AA standards, screen reader tools, and accessibility testing methodologies to fill a critical gap on the team
- Led UI refactoring effort across onshore and offshore teams to implement proper HTML5 semantic tags that screen readers could register
- Defined the ADA manifesto and development guidelines establishing accessibility standards for the portal across all client brands
- Trained development teams on accessibility principles and testing practices to ensure compliance was sustainable beyond the initial effort
- Designed and implemented custom templates tailored to client-specific branding and operational needs
Result: - Delivered ADA/AODA-compliant portal across all client brands, mitigating significant legal and regulatory exposure
- Development teams independently maintained compliance on subsequent releases without requiring external accessibility expertise
- Platform scaled to support Home Depot, Citi, Meijer, and Best Buy with distinct branding and compliance requirements from a single codebase, significantly reducing per-client customization effort

5PSummary: Fiserv's white-label card portal — the shared platform behind branded credit card sites for retailers like Home Depot, Citi, and Meijer — needed to serve each client with unique branding and compliance requirements. But the DOJ was aggressively targeting financial portals for ADA compliance, Canada's AODA deadline had already passed, and the team had no accessibility expertise. While serving as Development Manager on the overall program, I took on the ADA SME role — leading UI refactoring to proper HTML5 semantic markup, defining the accessibility manifesto, and training teams on compliance standards. The portal achieved compliance across all client brands, mitigating significant regulatory exposure.

Title: Driving Agile Transformation for Fiserv's Card Portal
Use Case(s): Transform waterfall delivery models into agile when long release cycles cause missed deadlines and threatened contracts. Coach teams through agile adoption where lack of visibility into work status creates stakeholder friction.
Interview Questions: Tell me about transforming a 45-person globally dispersed team from waterfall to agile?, Describe how you overcame compliance resistance to agile in a regulated financial services environment?, Walk me through decomposing a 200-page BRD into user stories and shifting how work was defined?, How did you establish follow-the-sun coordination for agile ceremonies across time zones?, Tell me about introducing sprint demos to banking stakeholders who were used to steering committee reporting?
Situation: Fiserv's $8.5M white-label card portal — serving enterprise clients like Home Depot, Citi, and Meijer — followed waterfall methodologies with 6-month release cycles. The 45-person globally dispersed team had no visibility into work status, requirements were defined in 200-page BRDs organized by system modules, and the project had missed multiple consecutive deadlines. Client stakeholders were threatening contract cancellation.
Task: Migrate the full team from waterfall to agile — introducing sprint cadence, restructuring how work was defined and tracked, and getting banking stakeholders comfortable with iterative delivery.
Action: - Transitioned a 45-person globally dispersed team from waterfall with 6-month release cycles to 2-week sprint cadence

- Restructured JIRA from a single To Do/Done board to a multi-tiered Kanban/Scrum architecture — tracking the 47 acceptance criteria as Epics with user stories flowing through specific workflow columns
- Established follow-the-sun synchronization — 7 AM CST scrum-of-scrums for offshore-to-onshore handoffs and 11 PM CST syncs for onshore-to-offshore
- Decomposed a 200-page BRD into user stories, shifting work definition from system modules to persona-based tasks
- Introduced bi-weekly sprint demos for Citi and Fiserv stakeholders — forcing early sign-off on branding, UI, and ADA compliance instead of discovering issues at month 6
- Maintained existing steering committee reporting while layering in sprint-level visibility for decision makers
- Coached offshore teams on empowerment over command-and-control — including training on screen reader audits (JAWS/NVDA) as part of Definition of Done

- Overcame PMO and compliance resistance to agile by using JIRA as system of record — demonstrating that audit trails, attached ADA scans, and developer comments were better for compliance than static Word documents
- Negotiated dedicated review windows with stakeholders who lacked bandwidth for bi-weekly feedback cycles to prevent sprint velocity from stalling
Result: - Transitioned 45-person team to 2-week sprint cadence within 3 months
- JIRA implementation provided real-time visibility to 50+ stakeholders across 3 time zones, replacing static steering committee reporting
- Bi-weekly sprint demos caught branding and ADA compliance issues early instead of at month 6
- JIRA as system of record satisfied PMO and compliance audit requirements while enabling agile delivery
- Offshore teams adopted empowerment model with screen reader audits built into Definition of Done

Title: Modernizing DevOps and Deployment for Fiserv's White-Label Card Portal
Use Case(s): Modernize DevOps practices from chaotic manual processes to disciplined CI/CD pipelines across globally dispersed teams. Establish code quality standards where none exist and implement build systems that enable predictable, SOX-compliant releases.
Interview Questions: Tell me about modernizing a build pipeline from SVN to Git in a regulated financial services environment?, Describe how you established code quality standards on a team that had none?, Walk me through implementing continuous integration under SOX constraints?, How did you reduce change failure rates on a platform serving 2M+ cardholders?, Tell me about migrating a globally dispersed team's source control while maintaining delivery momentum?
Situation: Fiserv's white-label card portal — a Java EE/Spring application serving multiple enterprise client brands on top of VisionPLUS 350 mainframe processing — was in a state of DevOps chaos. The team was using SVN for source control, and merging code between Omaha onshore and offshore teams regularly caused tree conflicts that halted builds. There was no continuous integration — builds took a day or longer wrangling dependencies. Deployments to production happened less than once per month, change failure rates were high, and the team frequently needed hotfixes or WAR rollbacks. Code quality was poor with no enforced review process or automated testing.
Task: Modernize the build and deployment pipeline to enable predictable, quality-controlled releases across parallel client brands while operating within SOX separation of duties constraints.
Action: - Migrated source control from SVN to Git/GitHub, eliminating the tree conflict bottleneck that was halting builds between onshore and offshore teams
- Established mandatory code review process — all code reviewed by a senior developer before merge, no exceptions
- Mandated unit test implementation aligned to backlog stories as a prerequisite for code completion
- Standardized the build pipeline using Apache Maven with proper POM structure and Artifactory for dependency management — reducing build times from days to hours
- Implemented continuous integration using Hudson, automating WAR file builds and enabling faster feedback on code quality
- Introduced SonarQube scans and JUnit testing as quality gates in the build pipeline
- Developed backup and rollback procedures for WAR deployments to manage release risk
- Coordinated release schedules across globally dispersed teams within SOX-compliant outage windows
Result: - Lead time improved from days-to-weeks down to one day to one week
- Deploy frequency increased from less than once per month to weekly-to-monthly cycles
- Change failure rate dropped significantly — from frequent hotfixes and WAR rollbacks to predictable, quality-controlled releases
- Git migration and code review process eliminated the SVN merge conflicts that had been blocking builds
- Unit testing and SonarQube scans established a quality baseline the team didn't have before
